proposals.title: Constrained Sparse Regression for Interpretable Single-Cell Phenotype Modeling
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, modeling patient outcomes or treatment responses from high-dimensional cell-type proportions requires interpretable, biologically plausible models. Standard penalized regression (e.g., lasso) can shrink coefficients but cannot enforce explicit prior knowledge, such as the non-negativity of certain immune cell contributions or monotonic relationships between tumor cell abundance and disease severity. Integrating such linear inequality constraints directly into the estimation framework would yield more reliable and interpretable models for precision oncology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating linear inequality constraints (e.g., β ≥ 0, Aβ ≤ b) into the coordinate descent algorithm for sparse regression will produce models with superior biological fidelity and predictive stability in clinical single-cell datasets, without compromising computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We will extend the cyclic coordinate descent algorithm for the lasso and elastic net to handle linear inequality constraints. For each coefficient update, we will solve a one-dimensional box-constrained or quadratic programming subproblem, ensuring the update respects all active constraints. The algorithm will be integrated into the `glmnet` ecosystem, with efficient warm-start strategies for pathwise computation. We will validate the method on single-cell RNA-seq datasets where features are cell-type abundances and labels are clinical outcomes (e.g., survival, treatment response).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, open-source tool for fitting sparse regression models with domain-specific constraints, directly applicable to biomarker discovery from single-cell data. It will enable researchers to embed prior biological knowledge (e.g., known promoter/inhibitor relationships) into predictive models, leading to more interpretable and clinically actionable signatures.
proposals.novelty: While penalty-based regularization is standard, the explicit integration of general linear inequality constraints into coordinate descent for sparse generalized linear models is underexplored. This moves beyond simple non-negativity constraints to allow for systematic encoding of biological pathways and regulatory relationships within the regression framework.

proposals.title: SCAD-Penalized Sparse Regression for Interpretable, Annotation-Free Discovery of Disease-Relevant Cell States
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying cell populations associated with disease or treatment response is critical for precision medicine. Current supervised and annotation-free methods often rely on L1 (lasso) regularization for feature selection, which can be biased, selecting too many irrelevant features and shrinking large coefficients excessively. Non-convex penalties like the Smoothly Clipped Absolute Deviation (SCAD) penalty, proposed by Fan and Li (2001), offer the oracle property—asymptotically unbiased estimates with correct variable selection—but have not been systematically integrated into modern frameworks for cell-state discovery from high-dimensional single-cell data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the SCAD penalty into a univariate-guided, sparse regression framework for single-cell data will yield more accurate and stable identification of disease-relevant cell states and gene programs compared to standard lasso-based approaches, while maintaining computational tractability.
proposals.method: We will extend the methodology from 'Annotation-free discovery of disease-relevant cells' and 'Univariate-Guided Sparse Regression'. The core algorithm will replace the L1 penalty with the SCAD penalty within a coordinate descent optimization routine, adapted for the single-cell regression setting where features are cells or gene programs and outcomes are patient-level labels (e.g., disease status, treatment response). We will develop a computational strategy to handle the non-convexity, likely employing local linear approximation or a majorization-minimization algorithm. Model performance will be validated on simulated data and public single-cell datasets from cancer and immunology, benchmarking against lasso and elastic net.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, statistically principled tool for biomarker discovery in single-cell biology, reducing false positive selections and improving interpretability. It will bridge a classic statistical advancement in high-dimensional regression with cutting-edge computational biology, offering a practical method for researchers to pinpoint cell states driving clinical phenotypes with greater confidence.
proposals.novelty: While SCAD is well-known in statistics, its application to the specific problem of annotation-free cell-state discovery in single-cell data, particularly within a univariate-guided framework that leverages patient-level outcomes, is novel and non-trivial, requiring careful algorithmic design for scalability and stability.

proposals.title: Distributed Cyclical Coordinate Descent for High-Dimensional Single-Cell and Genomic Regression
proposals.motivation: The analysis of modern single-cell and genomic datasets, which measure features across millions of cells from hundreds of patients, presents a severe computational bottleneck for fitting sparse regression models (e.g., lasso, elastic net). These models are essential for identifying cell states, biomarkers, and treatment-response signatures from high-dimensional data. The standard cyclical coordinate descent (CCD) algorithm, while efficient, is inherently sequential and struggles with datasets exceeding the memory and compute capacity of a single machine, limiting its application in large-scale biomedical studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed distributed implementation of CCD, which partitions features across workers and employs efficient synchronization, can achieve near-linear speedup while preserving the exact convergence properties and sparsity patterns of the serial algorithm for convex penalized regressions.
proposals.method: We will develop a distributed CCD framework using a data-parallel architecture (e.g., Apache Spark or Dask). Features (genes/proteins) will be partitioned column-wise across workers, with each worker storing a subset of the design matrix and responsible for updating its assigned coefficients. A central coordinator will manage the global parameter vector and intercept. To mitigate communication overhead, we will implement a 'residual broadcasting' strategy and explore hybrid parallelization (threading within nodes, message passing between nodes). Convergence diagnostics and safe, lock-free updates for the elastic net penalty will be core components, ensuring numerical equivalence to the glmnet algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, open-source software tool enabling the application of sparse regression to datasets with millions of observations and hundreds of thousands of features, directly benefiting single-cell and tumor genomics. It will establish best practices for parallelizing iterative optimization in statistics, bridging the gap between statistical methodology and high-performance computing. The method will facilitate the discovery of disease-relevant cell populations and predictive models from atlas-scale datasets, a key challenge in pre-2026 computational biology.
proposals.novelty: While parallel stochastic gradient descent is common, parallelizing exact, deterministic CCD for the lasso/elastic net with guaranteed equivalence to the canonical serial solution is non-trivial and less explored. Our approach innovates by directly adapting the robust, widely-used glmnet cycle within a distributed framework, prioritizing statistical fidelity over purely computational speed.

proposals.title: Graph-Guided Regularization Paths for Interpretable Single-Cell Biomarker Discovery in Cancer
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying cell populations associated with disease or treatment response is critical for precision oncology. Current methods, including the lasso, often select features (genes) independently, ignoring the known spatial or functional relationships between cells or genes, which can lead to biologically incoherent signatures. Incorporating structural knowledge during model fitting is essential for producing interpretable and robust biomarkers from high-dimensional single-cell data.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating graph-based penalties, such as the fused lasso or graph-guided fused lasso, into the generalized lasso path algorithm will yield more biologically interpretable and stable sets of predictive features by encouraging selection of spatially or functionally contiguous groups of cells or genes in single-cell datasets.
proposals.method: We will extend the GLM path algorithm (Park & Hastie, 2007) to efficiently compute regularization paths for models with generalized fused lasso penalties. This involves developing a solution path algorithm that handles the structured sparsity induced by a graph Laplacian penalty, where the graph encodes cell-cell similarity from single-cell embeddings or gene-gene networks. The method will be implemented in an open-source R package, with applications to annotated single-cell RNA-seq datasets from tumor and immune cells to predict patient-level labels like disease subtype or treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool for structured feature selection in single-cell biology, directly linking Tibshirani's foundational work on regularization paths with modern spatial and graph-based analysis needs. It will enable the discovery of coherent cellular neighborhoods or gene programs predictive of clinical outcomes, advancing beyond univariate or independent multivariate methods.
proposals.novelty: While graph penalties exist in image processing and genomics, their integration into the efficient, exact regularization path framework for GLMs is novel. This approach moves beyond the recent focus on annotation-free cell discovery by providing a supervised, penalty-driven method that directly incorporates topological constraints into the predictive model for patient-level outcomes.

proposals.title: A Dual Coordinate Ascent Algorithm for the Elastic Net GLM: Application to Interpretable Feature Selection in Single-Cell and Clinical Oncology
proposals.motivation: The elastic net generalized linear model (GLM) is a cornerstone for high-dimensional data analysis in genomics and clinical oncology, enabling sparse, interpretable feature selection for predicting patient outcomes or cell states. However, efficient optimization for large-scale problems, such as single-cell RNA-seq datasets with millions of cells or multi-modal patient data, remains a computational challenge. Solving the dual problem can offer algorithmic advantages, including simpler constraints and efficient screening rules, but a dedicated, scalable coordinate ascent algorithm for the elastic net GLM dual is not well-established.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully designed coordinate ascent algorithm applied to the dual of the elastic net GLM will yield faster convergence and greater computational efficiency for large-scale biological datasets than standard primal solvers, while maintaining identical statistical performance. This efficiency will enable more refined model exploration and feature discovery in complex datasets.
proposals.method: We will formally derive the dual problem for the elastic net-penalized GLM (including Gaussian, binomial, and Poisson families). We will develop a cyclic coordinate ascent algorithm that updates one dual variable at a time, exploiting the separable structure of the dual objective's constraints. Key innovations will include efficient updates using the link function's inverse, a warm-start strategy for pathwise regularization, and the integration of safe feature screening rules to pre-eliminate irrelevant variables (features, genes, or clinical markers). The algorithm will be implemented in an open-source R package, with benchmarking against `glmnet` on simulated and real single-cell and tumor genomics data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a new, efficient, and theoretically grounded optimization tool for high-dimensional GLMs, directly applicable to problems in single-cell analysis (e.g., identifying disease-relevant cell states) and clinical prediction (e.g., linking tumor features to treatment response). The dual perspective may also yield new insights into variable selection consistency and the development of enhanced screening rules, reducing computational burden for massive datasets.
proposals.novelty: While coordinate descent for the primal elastic net is standard (as in `glmnet`), a dedicated coordinate ascent algorithm for its GLM dual is novel. The integration of this solver with a focus on large-scale, annotation-free biological discovery—aligning with Tibshirani's recent work on interpretable supervised learning—represents a non-obvious and impactful synthesis of optimization theory and computational biology.

proposals.title: Warm-Start Regularization Paths from Unpenalized Solutions: Stability and Interpretability for Single-Cell and Clinical Models
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biological data, such as single-cell RNA-seq or patient-level genomic profiles, fitting sparse regression models (e.g., lasso) is crucial for identifying features, cells, or genes linked to disease, immune response, or treatment outcome. Standard algorithms compute the regularization path from a null model, which can be unstable in high-correlation settings, leading to variable feature selection and reduced interpretability. A more stable path could improve the reliability of discovered disease-relevant cell states and predictive biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initializing the coordinate descent algorithm for penalized regression (e.g., lasso, elastic net) at the unpenalized maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) or a ridge-penalized solution will produce a more stable and continuous regularization path, leading to more reproducible feature selection in high-dimensional, correlated biological datasets.
proposals.method: We will extend the glmnet algorithm to allow warm starts from non-penalized or weakly penalized (ridge) solutions. The core technical approach involves modifying the path-wise coordinate descent to begin at lambda=0 with the full MLE (or a ridge solution if MLE is undefined) and incrementally increase the penalty, using the previous solution as the starting point. We will implement this for logistic and Cox regression models, central to patient outcome prediction. Stability will be rigorously assessed via bootstrap resampling on single-cell and tumor genomics datasets, measuring the variance in selected features and prediction error across samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source software enhancement to glmnet, offering more stable feature selection for the biomedical community. It will yield a methodological framework for assessing algorithmic stability in penalized regression, directly benefiting research in annotation-free cell discovery and supervised learning for disease subtyping. The improved stability will enhance the interpretability and trust in models identifying key cells or patient features.
proposals.novelty: While warm starts are common in machine learning, systematically using the unpenalized MLE as the origin for the *entire* regularization path and formally evaluating its impact on stability in biological feature selection is novel. This bridges the conceptual gap between univariate-guided methods and full multivariate penalized regression, a natural extension of Tibshirani's recent work on guided sparse modeling and attention in supervised learning.

proposals.title: Sparse, Interpretable Prediction of Molecular Tumor Subtypes from Routine Clinical and Histopathological Features
proposals.motivation: Molecular subtyping of cancers (e.g., via RNA-seq) is critical for prognosis and treatment selection but remains costly and unavailable in many clinical settings. A robust method to infer these subtypes from widely available, low-cost clinical and histopathological data would dramatically increase access to precision oncology insights. This addresses a key translational gap, especially for resource-limited environments where genomic profiling is not feasible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparse, supervised learning model can identify a minimal, interpretable set of routine clinical and histopathological features that accurately predict canonical molecular subtypes of solid tumors, and that these predictive features will reveal biologically and clinically relevant associations with the underlying disease biology.
proposals.method: We will leverage large, integrated cancer datasets (e.g., TCGA) containing paired molecular subtype labels and rich clinical/histopathological features. The core model will be a sparse multinomial regression (e.g., group lasso or a univariate-guided sparse regression variant) to perform feature selection and prediction simultaneously, ensuring interpretability. We will extend this with an attention-based neural network layer (inspired by 'Supervised learning pays attention') to model interactions between selected features. Model performance will be validated on held-out cohorts and benchmarked against black-box alternatives.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a clinically deployable, interpretable tool for molecular subtyping prediction, directly addressing translational needs. Methodologically, it will advance the integration of sparse regression with attention mechanisms for biological data. The identified sparse feature set will provide novel hypotheses about the clinical correlates of molecular subtypes, offering biological insight beyond prediction.
proposals.novelty: While previous work predicts outcomes from molecular data, the inverse problem—predicting molecular state from routine clinical data using sparse, interpretable models—is less explored. The novelty lies in framing this as a high-dimensional, sparse inference problem tailored for clinical translation, combining Tibshirani's expertise in sparsity with modern attention mechanisms for biological interpretability.

proposals.title: Robust Selective Inference for High-Dimensional Biomarker Discovery via Influence Functions and the Wild Bootstrap
proposals.motivation: Selective inference is crucial for valid post-selection inference in high-dimensional genomic and clinical datasets, but standard methods rely heavily on Gaussian error assumptions. In practice, single-cell RNA-seq data, patient survival times, and tumor microenvironment features often exhibit heavy-tailed or skewed error distributions, violating these assumptions and leading to unreliable p-values and confidence intervals for discovered biomarkers or cell types. This gap limits the robustness of findings in cancer genomics and immunology.
proposals.hypothesis: By characterizing the asymptotic influence function of post-selection estimators and employing a tailored resampling scheme, we can construct valid selective confidence intervals and p-values that are robust to non-Gaussian, heteroskedastic error structures common in biological data.
proposals.method: We will derive the asymptotic distribution of estimators after model selection (e.g., via the lasso) using their influence function representation, explicitly conditioning on the selection event. This characterization will inform the design of a model-robust bootstrap procedure, specifically a wild bootstrap with appropriate weighting to replicate the estimator's influence curve under the selection constraint. The method will be implemented and tested on simulated data with t-distributed and skewed errors, and applied to real single-cell datasets for disease-relevant cell discovery and patient outcome prediction from tumor microenvironment features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for robust selective inference, directly applicable to genomic and clinical datasets where Gaussian assumptions fail. It will extend the reliability of supervised learning and sparse regression methods in Tibshirani's recent work to a broader class of error distributions, strengthening the validity of biological conclusions about immune cells, treatment effects, and disease mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While influence functions and the bootstrap are established in robust statistics and econometrics, their integration into the selective inference framework to handle non-parametric errors post-selection is novel. This approach moves beyond current post-selection inference methods, which are primarily designed for Gaussian settings.

proposals.title: Valid Post-Selection Inference for Single-Cell Biomarker Discovery with Decoupled Correlation Estimation
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying cell populations or gene features associated with disease or treatment response is a core supervised learning task. Standard post-selection inference (PSI) methods, like selective inference for the lasso, assume known or simple feature covariance structures, which are rarely accurate in high-dimensional biological data where features (genes) exhibit complex, unknown correlations. This leads to invalid p-values and false discoveries when selecting disease-relevant cells or immune features, undermining reliable biological interpretation.
proposals.hypothesis: By rigorously decoupling the estimation of the feature correlation structure from the model selection event using a data-splitting framework, we can construct valid conditional confidence intervals and p-values for selected features that are robust to misspecification of the true dependency structure.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-split algorithm where an independent subset of cells is used solely to estimate the gene-gene covariance matrix via a regularized method (e.g., graphical lasso). This estimate then defines the constraints for a model selection procedure (e.g., the lasso or a forward selection variant) applied to a separate data subset. Finally, we will employ a conditional testing framework, extending the polyhedral lemma approach, to compute valid inference for the selected features, explicitly accounting for the randomness in the initial covariance estimation. The method will be tailored for single-cell RNA-seq data, where 'features' are genes and 'samples' are cells or patients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous tool for biomarker discovery in single-cell and bulk genomic studies, ensuring reliable identification of disease-relevant cell states or treatment-responsive genes. It directly addresses a key limitation in applying selective inference to omics data, bridging the gap between high-dimensional statistics and practical computational biology. The resulting software will enhance the reproducibility of findings in immuno-oncology and other fields.
proposals.novelty: Existing PSI methods treat the covariance as known or estimate it from the same data used for selection, creating bias. Our core innovation is the formal decoupling and explicit conditioning on the correlation estimation step within the selection event, a problem not addressed by prior selective inference or data-splitting work.

proposals.title: Whitening for Selection: Valid Post-Selection Inference in High-Dimensional, Correlated Feature Spaces
proposals.motivation: Valid inference after feature selection is a critical unsolved problem in high-dimensional biology, where features like gene expression are highly correlated. Existing post-selection inference (PoSI) methods often assume feature independence, leading to invalid p-values and confidence intervals when applied to correlated genomic or single-cell data, misleading biological interpretation and clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the Karhunen–Loève (KL) transform, a geophysical signal processing technique for decorrelating sensor noise, will enable valid post-selection inference for correlated features by creating an approximately orthogonal space where standard PoSI theory applies.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where the empirical feature correlation matrix is estimated from the data and decomposed via eigen-decomposition. This defines a whitening transform applied to the design matrix. Model selection (e.g., the Lasso) is performed in this whitened space, followed by the application of PoSI methods like selective inference. Finally, the resulting confidence intervals and p-values are transformed back to the original, biologically interpretable feature space using the inverse transform, ensuring validity accounts for the initial correlation structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, theoretically grounded method for performing valid inference after selection in correlated settings common in genomics. It will directly enable more reliable identification of disease-relevant genes or cell states from high-dimensional datasets, improving the robustness of biomarker discovery for patient stratification and treatment response prediction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual and technical cross-pollination from geophysical signal processing to high-dimensional statistics, specifically using the KL transform to 'trick' selection algorithms into operating on orthogonalized features, a non-obvious solution to the correlated feature problem in PoSI.

proposals.title: Correlation-Aware Post-Selection Inference for High-Dimensional Biomarker Discovery
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biological data, such as single-cell or bulk tumor genomics, features (e.g., gene expression) are highly correlated. Existing selection-adjusted inference methods, like the selective inference framework for the lasso, often assume features are independent or have a known, fixed covariance. This leads to overly conservative or anti-conservative p-values and confidence intervals when applied to real, correlated data, undermining the reliable identification of disease-relevant cells or patient subtypes for treatment stratification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling and incorporating the empirical feature correlation structure into the selection event characterization, we can develop inference tools that yield valid and more powerful p-values and confidence intervals after feature selection in correlated settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the polyhedral lemma framework for post-selection inference to condition on both the selected model and the observed empirical correlation matrix. The core technical challenge is to derive the exact conditional distribution of the test statistic given selection under a Gaussian model with an estimated covariance. We will employ a parametric bootstrap or a decorrelation approach based on the Cholesky decomposition of the estimated covariance matrix to approximate this distribution. The method will be integrated with sparse regression (lasso) and group-lasso penalties, tailored for applications where features naturally group, such as genes in pathways or cells within clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source software package for performing valid inference after model selection in correlated high-dimensional data. It will directly benefit the analysis of single-cell datasets for annotation-free discovery of disease-relevant immune cells and the identification of robust genomic biomarkers for patient stratification, leading to more reliable scientific conclusions.
proposals.novelty: While post-selection inference is an established field, existing tools largely ignore or simplify the problem of estimated, non-identity feature correlation. Our approach directly tackles this gap by conditioning on the observed correlation structure, moving beyond the independent features assumption that is routinely violated in biological applications.

proposals.title: Valid Post-Selection Inference for Sparse Models in High-Dimensional Biomedical Data
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biomedical studies, such as single-cell or tumor genomics, sparse regression models like the lasso are essential for identifying disease-relevant features (e.g., genes or cell states). However, standard practice uses data-driven cross-validation to select the penalty parameter, which introduces a selection bias. This invalidates subsequent p-values and confidence intervals for the selected features, undermining reliable biological inference and clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a conditional inference framework, accounting for the exact selection event of choosing the lasso penalty via cross-validation, can yield valid post-selection p-values and confidence intervals for features in high-dimensional biological data.
proposals.method: We will extend the selective inference framework (e.g., Lee et al., 2016) to condition on the event that a specific penalty parameter λ̂ is chosen by K-fold cross-validation. This involves characterizing the polyhedral selection region for the cross-validation error curve's minimum. We will develop efficient algorithms to compute exact conditional distributions for the lasso coefficients post-CV selection, leveraging recent advances in truncated Gaussian sampling. The method will be validated on simulated data and applied to real single-cell RNA-seq datasets to identify immune cell states associated with treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous inferential tool for sparse models after data-driven hyperparameter tuning, directly addressing a major gap in applied statistics. It will enable biologists to perform valid hypothesis tests on features selected via standard CV pipelines, increasing the reliability of discoveries in genomics and immunology.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference for the lasso given a fixed λ exists, conditioning on the stochastic event of CV-based λ selection is a novel and significantly more complex problem. This approach integrates the data-driven model selection workflow ubiquitous in practice with formal statistical inference.

proposals.title: Selective Conformal Inference for Robust Prediction of Patient Immune Response from Single-Cell Data
proposals.motivation: In modern translational oncology, single-cell RNA sequencing enables the identification of disease-relevant immune cell states predictive of patient treatment response. However, the complex, adaptive workflows used to select predictive features or models from this high-dimensional data invalidate standard statistical inference, leading to overconfident and unreliable predictions. A model-agnostic framework for valid uncertainty quantification after data-driven model selection is urgently needed to support clinical decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by decoupling model selection and inference via data splitting, and applying a conformal calibration step conditioned on the held-out selection set, we can achieve prediction intervals with valid marginal coverage that approximate conditional validity for the selected model, even for black-box selection procedures common in single-cell analysis.
proposals.method: We will adapt the proposed Model-Agnostic Selective Conformal Inference framework to the single-cell domain. The workflow will: 1) Split data into selection, calibration, and test sets. 2) Apply a black-box selection procedure (e.g., annotation-free discovery of disease-relevant cell clusters using supervised attention mechanisms) on the selection set to train a final predictive model for a clinical outcome. 3) Treat this model as fixed and use the calibration set to compute conformal prediction intervals via split conformal prediction or its jackknife+ variant. 4) Derive theoretical guarantees for marginal coverage and investigate practical conditional validity through careful conditioning on the selection set's characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for generating reliable prediction intervals for complex biomarkers derived from adaptive single-cell analysis, directly addressing the post-selection inference problem in computational biology. It will enable more statistically rigorous translation of single-cell discoveries into clinical prognostic tools for patient stratification and treatment prediction.
proposals.novelty: While conformal prediction is established, its integration with and conditioning on the outcome of adaptive, black-box model selection procedures—common in modern single-cell workflows—remains unexplored, moving beyond traditional post-selection inference methods that require explicit characterization of the selection event.

proposals.title: Ensemble Calibration for Valid Post-Selection Inference in Single-Cell and Clinical Prediction Models
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biomedical data, such as single-cell RNA-seq or clinical omics, researchers use complex, non-linear models (e.g., trees, neural networks) to identify disease-relevant cells or predict patient outcomes. Standard inference after this model selection is invalid, leading to overconfident p-values and unreliable confidence intervals for discovered features or treatment effects. This undermines reproducibility and clinical translation, creating a critical need for robust post-selection inference methods tailored to modern machine learning workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical calibration frameworks developed for climate ensemble forecasts can be adapted to correct for selection bias in non-linear models, yielding valid conditional inference after feature or model selection.
proposals.method: We will treat the output of a selection algorithm (e.g., a neural network's attention on specific cell types or genes) as a raw, uncalibrated ensemble. Using a dedicated calibration set, we will apply resampling-based perturbations (e.g., bootstrap, jackknife, or data-splitting) to the selection process to empirically estimate the bias and variance inflation of the selected model's test statistics. We will then develop a post-hoc calibration layer, inspired by Bayesian Model Averaging and conformal prediction, that adjusts p-values and confidence intervals to achieve nominal coverage, conditional on the selection event, for key parameters like gene expression effects or treatment coefficients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, model-agnostic tool for performing valid inference after complex selection in biomedical data analysis. It will directly enhance the reliability of findings from supervised learning pipelines that identify disease-relevant cells or predict immune response to treatment, strengthening the statistical foundation for translational discoveries.
proposals.novelty: While post-selection inference exists for linear models (e.g., selective inference for the lasso), and ensemble calibration is standard in climate science, the novel synthesis for non-linear biomedical models and the explicit analogy to forecast calibration is unexplored. This approach moves beyond data-splitting to a more efficient, resampling-based calibration framework.

proposals.title: Selective Inference for Adaptive, Non-Linear Model Selection in Genomic and AutoML Pipelines
proposals.motivation: In modern biomedical data science, workflows for identifying disease-relevant features, such as specific cell types or gene signatures from single-cell or tumor data, are increasingly complex. They often combine adaptive model selection (e.g., stability selection, genetic algorithms) with powerful non-linear learners (e.g., neural networks, tree ensembles). Existing selective inference methods, like those for the lasso, are insufficient for these composite, data-adaptive pipelines, leaving scientists without valid statistical guarantees for the discovered biomarkers or treatment-response predictors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a unified selective inference framework can be constructed to provide valid post-selection confidence intervals and p-values for features identified by any adaptive, non-linear model selection pipeline, by conditioning on the entire selection event.
proposals.method: We will extend the selective inference paradigm to condition on the output of a composite selection algorithm. The framework will treat the joint action of a model space search (e.g., a reinforcement learning agent or genetic algorithm) and a subsequent non-parametric fit (e.g., a random forest or shallow neural network) as a single selection event. We will develop computational methods to approximate the selection-adjusted sampling distribution, leveraging tools from approximate conditioning and randomized response. Validation will be performed on simulated genomic data and real single-cell RNA-seq datasets where the goal is to identify immune cell states associated with disease or treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous inferential toolkit for complex, automated discovery pipelines common in genomics and AutoML. It will enable researchers like Tibshirani to report statistically valid measures of uncertainty for features discovered through sophisticated, end-to-end adaptive analyses, bridging the gap between exploratory machine learning and confirmatory statistics.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference exists for linear models with convex selection, our framework is novel in its joint handling of discrete, combinatorial model search algorithms and highly flexible, non-parametric final models, which is a dominant paradigm in contemporary analysis.

proposals.title: Conditional Inference for Tree-Based Models in Single-Cell Genomics: Valid p-values for Cell Type and Gene Selection
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, researchers use tree-based models (e.g., random forests, gradient boosting) to identify cell subpopulations or gene features predictive of patient disease state or treatment response. However, standard feature importance scores from these models do not provide valid statistical inference, as they ignore the selection bias from both model fitting and the common practice of data-driven feature pre-screening. This leads to unreliable biological conclusions about which cells or genes are truly disease-relevant.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending the selective inference framework to condition on the entire model selection event of a tree-based algorithm, we can produce valid post-selection p-values and confidence intervals for the importance of selected genomic features, thereby controlling type I error.
proposals.method: We will adapt the polyhedral lemma approach, used for lasso selective inference, to the discrete selection events of recursive partitioning. The key is to characterize the conditioning set for a selected split (e.g., a specific gene exceeding a threshold in a node) as a union of polyhedra in the data space. We will develop efficient algorithms to compute these sets for CART-style trees and ensembles, leveraging recent work on attention in supervised learning to guide the conditioning on the most salient selection steps. The method will be implemented for common tasks like identifying differential abundance of immune cell types between tumor conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical selective inference tool for complex, non-linear models widely used in genomics, enabling statistically rigorous biomarker discovery from high-dimensional single-cell data. It will directly enhance the reliability of findings from 'annotation-free' cell discovery pipelines by adding formal inference after selection.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference exists for linear models, applying it to the highly non-linear and combinatorial selection events of tree-based models is a major unsolved challenge. Our novelty lies in formulating the tree-building process as a structured selection event and developing a computationally feasible conditioning scheme that accounts for the entire ensemble fitting procedure, not just a single split.

proposals.title: Caching Canonical Selection Events: A Lattice-Based Framework for Efficient Post-Selection Inference
proposals.motivation: Selective inference provides valid p-values after model selection but is computationally prohibitive for high-dimensional biomedical data, such as single-cell RNA-seq datasets where identifying disease-relevant cell states requires testing thousands of features. This computational bottleneck limits the real-time application of rigorous inference in clinical or research settings, such as assessing patient-specific treatment responses. Faster methods are needed to make selective inference practical for large-scale, interactive analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The polyhedral representations of common selection events (e.g., from the lasso or forward stepwise regression) possess discrete symmetries, and precomputing inference quantities for a library of canonical selection polyhedra will enable dramatic speed-ups via cache lookup and symmetry transformation, rather than de novo computation for each new dataset.
proposals.method: We will formalize the algebraic symmetries (sign flips, permutations, rotations) of selection polyhedra arising from common procedures like the lasso with fixed lambda. A library of canonical polyhedra and their associated truncated Gaussian integration constants or sampling distributions will be precomputed using existing Monte Carlo or numerical integration methods. For a new dataset, the algorithm will map the observed selection event to a canonical form via a symmetry group action, retrieve the cached inference quantities, and apply the inverse transformation to obtain valid p-values and confidence intervals, bypassing intensive integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a software package enabling real-time selective inference for high-dimensional problems, directly applicable to biomarker discovery from genomic data. It will provide a novel theoretical link between computational geometry, group theory, and statistical inference, extending the methodological toolkit for post-selection analysis.
proposals.novelty: While precomputation is used in other fields like crystallography, its application to selective inference via the symmetry structure of selection events is novel. This approach moves beyond optimizing a single algorithm to creating a reusable, lookup-based infrastructure for inference.

proposals.title: Fast Selective Inference for High-Dimensional Biomarker Discovery in Single-Cell and Clinical Data
proposals.motivation: Selective inference is crucial for providing valid p-values and confidence intervals after data-driven model selection, such as with the lasso. However, its computational cost, particularly the need for numerical integration over high-dimensional truncation regions, limits its application to modern high-throughput biological datasets. This bottleneck prevents its routine use in single-cell genomics and clinical biomarker studies, where researchers need to rapidly identify disease-relevant cell states or patient features from thousands of candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by exploiting the sparsity and structure inherent in high-dimensional biological feature selection problems, we can develop novel approximations and optimization strategies that dramatically accelerate selective inference calculations while maintaining statistical validity.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-pronged algorithmic approach. First, we will create a fast, univariate-guided approximation for the lasso's selection event, building on Tibshirani's recent work, to simplify the complex polyhedral truncation region. Second, we will implement a GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo sampler specifically designed for the conditional distributions arising in post-selection inference, leveraging modern parallel computing architectures. We will validate our methods on simulated data and real single-cell RNA-seq datasets from tumor and immune cell studies, where features are genes and 'labels' are disease or treatment status.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce an open-source software package that makes valid post-selection inference computationally feasible for large-scale genomic and clinical datasets. It will directly enable more rigorous biomarker discovery from high-dimensional data, allowing researchers to move quickly from 'training' models on data with 'disease' labels to reporting reliable confidence statements on selected 'cells' or 'patient' features.
proposals.novelty: While existing selective inference methods are generic, our approach is novel in its tight integration with the specific sparse, high-dimensional structure of biological data and its use of hardware acceleration, which has not been systematically applied to this statistical challenge.

proposals.title: Bayesian Selective Inference for Adaptive Modeling in High-Dimensional Biology
proposals.motivation: In modern biological studies, such as single-cell or tumor genomics, researchers use complex, data-adaptive pipelines (e.g., lasso with CV) to select features, cell types, or predictive models. Standard post-selection inference methods condition on a single selected model, ignoring the uncertainty introduced by the hyperparameter tuning and model selection process itself. This leads to overconfident inferences that do not honestly reflect the exploratory nature of the analysis, undermining reproducibility in patient stratification and biomarker discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating selective inference to condition on the entire adaptive selection event with Bayesian model averaging over the posterior distribution of candidate models given that event, we can produce a posterior distribution and credible intervals that are calibrated to account for both selection bias and model uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will extend the selective inference framework to condition on the joint event of model and hyperparameter selection (e.g., the specific lambda chosen by cross-validation). For the space of models visited during the adaptive procedure, we will construct a likelihood conditioned on this event. A prior over models will be updated to a posterior distribution using this selective likelihood. Final inference for parameters or predictions will be based on Bayesian model averaging across this posterior, yielding valid credible intervals. Computational strategies will leverage approximate sampling and polyhedral constraints from pre-2026 selective inference work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified framework for valid post-adaptive inference, delivering calibrated uncertainty estimates from complex analytical pipelines common in genomics and clinical prediction. It will directly enhance the reliability of findings in areas like disease-relevant cell discovery and treatment response prediction by honestly quantifying uncertainty after model selection and tuning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of the selective inference conditioning approach with Bayesian model averaging specifically over the posterior defined by the selection event, moving beyond conditioning on a single model to propagate the uncertainty of the entire adaptive path.

proposals.title: Controlling the 'Look-Elsewhere Effect' in Post-Selection Inference: Adapting Astrophysical Multiple Testing Corrections for High-Dimensional Biomedical Data
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biomedical studies, such as single-cell RNA-seq analysis for disease-relevant cells or genomic studies for tumor features, researchers first perform a data-driven selection step (e.g., variable selection, cluster identification) before conducting inference. Standard statistical methods ignore the selection bias from this initial search, leading to invalid p-values and false discoveries. This 'post-selection inference' problem is directly analogous to the 'look-elsewhere effect' in astrophysics, where scanning the sky for signals inflates false discovery rates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multiple testing correction frameworks developed in astrophysics and cosmology for correlated, high-dimensional searches—specifically tailored to control the Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER) and False Discovery Rate (FDR) under spatial correlation—can be successfully adapted and extended to provide valid post-selection inference in statistical and machine learning models common in biomedical research.
proposals.method: We will formalize the analogy between astrophysical sky surveys (e.g., searches for exoplanet transits in Kepler data) and biomedical feature selection (e.g., lasso regression for patient outcome prediction). We will adapt methods like the Benjamini-Yekutieli FDR procedure for correlated fields and the Gross & Vitells 'trial factor' correction for excursions in random fields. These will be integrated into a post-selection inference pipeline for sparse regression (e.g., the selective inference framework) and for annotation-free cell discovery in single-cell datasets, using permutation and simulation-based calibration to estimate the effective number of independent tests.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, domain-adapted solution for valid inference after model selection in high-dimensional biology, directly addressing a core limitation in modern data analysis. It will yield new software tools and methodological guidelines for controlling error rates in studies of tumor microenvironments, immune cell populations, and treatment response prediction, where selection is inherent.
proposals.novelty: While post-selection inference is an active area in statistics, and multiple testing is standard in genomics, the direct translation of specialized corrections from astrophysics—designed for spatially correlated, continuous searches—to this biomedical context is novel and non-obvious, offering a fresh perspective grounded in a different scientific discipline.

proposals.title: Stability Paths for Post-Selection Inference in High-Dimensional Genomic Models of Tumor-Immune Interactions
proposals.motivation: In single-cell and bulk genomic studies of cancer, identifying features (e.g., genes, cell states) predictive of patient disease outcome or treatment response requires variable selection. Standard post-selection inference methods condition on a single selected model, ignoring the inherent stochasticity of the selection process itself. This leads to overconfident inferences, especially in high-dimensional settings where many correlated features compete for inclusion, undermining the reliability of biological conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the distribution of selected models via resampling will yield more robust and interpretable measures of feature importance and uncertainty, leading to more stable biological signatures of tumor-immune biology.
proposals.method: We will develop a nested bootstrap-integrated stability selection algorithm. The outer loop generates perturbed datasets (e.g., by subsampling cells or patients). For each, an inner loop performs model selection (e.g., via the lasso or an attention-guided sparse regression from recent work) on further bootstrapped samples to compute selection probabilities. Aggregating across outer loops yields stability paths for each parameter—showing its selection probability as a function of regularization—and a final ensemble of selected models. Confidence intervals will be constructed from the distribution of parameter estimates across this ensemble, adjusted for selection frequency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, computationally efficient framework for post-selection inference that directly quantifies selection uncertainty. It will deliver more reliable feature sets and confidence intervals for biomarkers in oncology, enhancing the translation of high-dimensional genomic analyses into robust, disease-relevant biological insights.
proposals.novelty: While stability selection and the bootstrap exist, their integration into a unified inferential framework for post-selection confidence intervals, explicitly designed for the high-noise, high-correlation setting of single-cell and patient-level genomic data, is novel. This moves beyond conditioning on a single model or using naive bootstrap-after-selection.

proposals.title: Bootstrap Confidence for Selected Features: A Phylogenetic Analogy for Post-Selection Inference in High-Dimensional Biology
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying disease-relevant cell populations or gene features is a high-dimensional model selection problem, analogous to variable selection in regression. Standard inference after this data-driven selection is invalid, overstating confidence in discovered biomarkers or cell types. This undermines reproducible translation to patient stratification or treatment targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the phylogenetic bootstrap—which assesses confidence in tree branches after tree-building—to feature selection in biological data will yield valid, model-agnostic confidence measures for selected cells or genes, accounting for the selection process's variability.
proposals.method: We will apply this to single-cell datasets (e.g., from tumor or immune disease studies). After an initial selection of disease-relevant cells or features using a method like sparse regression or annotation-free discovery, we will generate non-parametric bootstrap samples of cells (or samples). The full selection algorithm will be re-run on each bootstrap sample. The frequency of selection for each feature (e.g., a cell cluster or gene) across bootstrap replicates provides a direct confidence score. We will develop metrics and intervals from this distribution, akin to bootstrap support values on phylogenetic trees.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a practical, computationally intensive framework for post-selection inference in exploratory biological data analysis, moving beyond asymptotic assumptions. It directly addresses the reproducibility crisis in biomarker discovery by quantifying the stability of selected biological features, aiding in the prioritization of targets for validation.
proposals.novelty: While the bootstrap is well-known, its specific adaptation as a post-selection confidence tool, explicitly inspired by its successful role in phylogenetics, is novel for single-cell biology. It offers a model-agnostic alternative to recent parametric post-selection inference methods in statistics.

proposals.title: Robust Causal Inference for Single-Cell Genomics: An Adversarial Framework with Integrated Sensitivity Analysis for Selection Bias
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomic studies of patient tumors, selection bias arises when profiled cells are not a random sample of the tumor microenvironment, distorting inferences about cell-type-specific treatment effects or disease mechanisms. Pre-2026 methods for causal adjustment rely on strong parametric assumptions about the selection mechanism and unmeasured confounding, which are often untenable in biological contexts, leading to overconfident and potentially biased conclusions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a flexible, adversarial selection model with a multi-dimensional sensitivity analysis will yield causal estimates for cell-state-specific outcomes (e.g., treatment response) that are more robust to model misspecification and unmeasured confounding than standard propensity-score or outcome-regression methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial framework. First, we train a flexible, non-parametric model (e.g., a gradient-boosted machine or neural network) to predict selection into the sample using all observed cell and patient features. Second, we use this model to generate stabilized inverse probability weights, which are then used in a robust, penalized regression (e.g., the lasso or an attention-based model) for the outcome. Crucially, we integrate a sensitivity analysis by adversarially perturbing the selection model's predictions across a pre-defined space of plausible unmeasured confounders, generating a map of causal estimates and their stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical tool for computational biologists to perform causal inference on single-cell data with explicit, quantifiable confidence intervals that account for selection bias. It will advance the field by moving beyond point estimates to a 'dashboard' of results under different violation scenarios, directly addressing the reproducibility crisis in high-dimensional biology.
proposals.novelty: The tight integration of adversarial machine learning for selection modeling with a multi-parameter sensitivity analysis, outputting a robustness map, is a novel synthesis beyond standard sensitivity approaches that vary one parameter at a time. This leverages Tibshirani's expertise in sparse regression and attention to model transparency within a modern causal inference workflow.

proposals.title: CLEAN-Select: Iterative Selective Inference for Ultra-High-Dimensional Biological Data via Astrophysical Signal Decomposition
proposals.motivation: In modern single-cell and genomic studies (p >> n), identifying disease-relevant features or cell states with valid statistical inference is critical for understanding tumor biology and treatment response. Current selective inference frameworks, while providing Type I error control, struggle with computational and statistical scalability in these ultra-high-dimensional settings, limiting their practical utility for large-scale discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iterative algorithm, inspired by the CLEAN method from radio astronomy for isolating discrete signals from noisy interferometric data, can provide a scalable and statistically valid framework for post-selection inference in ultra-high-dimensional biological datasets.
proposals.method: We will adapt the CLEAN paradigm into an iterative selective inference procedure. Each cycle will: 1) apply a high-dimensional screening method (e.g., marginal correlation or a lasso step) to the current residual data matrix to select the most promising feature; 2) conduct valid post-selection inference (e.g., using polyhedral lemma-based conditional testing) on that feature given the selection event; 3) orthogonalize the entire dataset (features and response) with respect to the selected feature to create a new residual matrix. This process repeats, controlling for selection at each step, to build a sequentially valid set of inferences and manage extreme dimensionality through iterative decomposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computationally feasible method for valid statistical inference after feature selection in p >> n problems, directly applicable to single-cell data for discovering disease-relevant cell states or patient-specific biomarkers. It will extend the theory of selective inference to iterative, greedy selection schemes with formal error control.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual and algorithmic translation of a signal decomposition framework from astrophysics (CLEAN) to solve a fundamental scalability bottleneck in selective inference, creating an iterative procedure distinct from standard one-shot penalized regression approaches.

proposals.title: Post-Selection Inference for Sparse Single-Cell Signatures in Ultra-High-Dimensional Disease Profiling
proposals.motivation: In modern single-cell genomics, datasets routinely measure gene expression in hundreds of thousands of cells from individual patients, creating an ultra-high-dimensional feature space (genes) vastly exceeding the sample size (patients). This is critical for identifying rare, disease-relevant cell states that predict treatment response or immune activity, but standard selective inference methods for lasso-like procedures fail to scale computationally or statistically in this p >> n regime. Valid p-values for discovered cellular signatures are essential for reproducible translational research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging the intrinsic low-dimensional structure of single-cell data—specifically, that informative signals are sparse and reside in coordinated gene programs—we can develop a computationally tractable selective inference framework that provides valid confidence intervals for selected features even when p is orders of magnitude larger than n.
proposals.method: We will extend the polyhedral lemma and approximate MLE approaches for post-selection inference to handle ultra-high-dimensional settings. The core technical innovation will be a two-stage procedure: first, use a univariate-guided screening step (inspired by 'Univariate-Guided Sparse Regression') to reduce dimensionality to a manageable scale, then apply a randomized lasso or elastic net for stable selection. Inference will be conducted conditional on both the screening and selection events, using recent advances in high-dimensional asymptotic theory and efficient matrix computations for the polyhedral constraints. We will validate the method on simulated data and single-cell RNA-seq datasets with patient-level labels for disease outcome or treatment response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first practical selective inference tool for ultra-high-dimensional biological datasets, enabling researchers to assign statistical confidence to cell-type-specific gene signatures predictive of clinical outcomes. It will directly enhance the reliability of discoveries from 'annotation-free' cell state detection methods by quantifying the uncertainty of their associations with patient phenotypes.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference is established for moderate-dimensional regression, scaling it to the p>>n regime of single-cell genomics by integrating feature screening with valid conditional inference is a novel and non-trivial challenge. The method uniquely bridges Tibshirani's foundational work on sparse modeling with the practical demands of modern, massive-scale cellular phenotyping.

proposals.title: Online Selective Inference for Streaming Single-Cell Data: Valid p-values for Dynamically Selected Immune Cell Populations
proposals.motivation: In modern single-cell immunology, data arrives as a stream from longitudinal patient samples, and researchers continuously screen for cell populations correlated with treatment response or disease progression. Standard selective inference methods, which correct for the bias of data-driven model selection, are designed for static datasets and cannot provide valid sequential p-values as new data and hypotheses arrive. This creates a crisis of reproducibility in dynamic analyses of tumor microenvironments and immune dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting the selective inference framework to an online setting with algorithmic filtering, we can construct conditionally valid confidence intervals and p-values for features (e.g., gene expression in a cell type) selected from a streaming data sequence, controlling for the cumulative selection bias.
proposals.method: We will extend the polyhedral lemma approach of selective inference to handle an online sequence of model selection events. The core method will involve maintaining a running 'selection region' conditioned on past data and selection decisions. For a data stream (e.g., single-cell batches from a patient over time), we will integrate an online lasso or sequential attention mechanism to flag disease-relevant cell states. Upon selection, we will compute the exact conditional null distribution of the selected coefficient given the online selection history, using pre-2026 online convex optimization techniques to manage the evolving constraint set efficiently.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous inferential tool for scientists analyzing streaming single-cell or clinical data, enabling real-time, statistically valid discovery of biomarkers and cell types. It will directly enhance the reliability of dynamic analyses in immunology and oncology. The software implementation will make online selective inference accessible for high-throughput biological data streams.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference is established for static data, and online learning is mature, their synthesis for valid *sequential* inference after data-dependent selection is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc correction to a framework for live, statistically guarded scientific discovery.

proposals.title: Federated Selective Inference: Provably Valid Post-Selection Confidence Intervals for Distributed Model Selection in High-Dimensional Biology
proposals.motivation: Modern single-cell and tumor genomics studies require distributed computation for model training and feature selection across decentralized patient data, driven by privacy (e.g., federated learning) and scale. However, standard selective inference frameworks assume a centralized, deterministic selection path, ignoring how distributed optimization algorithms (like ADMM or federated averaging) introduce new, stochastic algorithmic selection events. This creates a critical gap: confidence intervals for selected immune cell markers or disease-relevant features may be invalid when selection is entangled with distributed computation.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally characterizing the conditional selection event induced by the iterative consensus and aggregation steps of distributed algorithms, we can co-design the algorithmic splitting strategy and the inference adjustment to produce provably valid post-selection confidence intervals in a decentralized setting.
proposals.method: We will extend the selective inference framework to model the algorithmic path of consensus ADMM and federated gradient methods as a sequence of data-dependent constraints. For a high-dimensional sparse regression model (e.g., the lasso), we will derive the exact polyhedral selection region conditioned on both the initial sparsity pattern and the consensus history across nodes. The computational architecture will use a master node to compute the adjusted inference via truncated Gaussian sampling, using only aggregated sufficient statistics from client nodes holding individual patient or cell data, ensuring privacy. Validation will use synthetic data and real single-cell RNA-seq datasets with simulated distributed partitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous method for valid hypothesis testing after model selection in federated and distributed computing environments. It will deliver practical software enabling biologists to obtain reliable confidence intervals for disease-associated genes or cell types discovered from decentralized datasets without centralizing raw data.
proposals.novelty: While selective inference is established, and distributed algorithms are mature, their intersection is unexplored. The novelty lies in treating the algorithmic decentralization mechanism as a fundamental part of the selection event, not merely a computational detail, thereby ensuring statistical validity is preserved by design.

proposals.title: Supervised Parameter Selection for Single-Cell Data Imputation Using Disease and Treatment Labels
proposals.motivation: Single-cell RNA sequencing data is plagued by dropout events (false zeros), and imputation methods like KNNimpute and SVDimpute are widely used to correct them. However, their performance is highly sensitive to user-chosen parameters (e.g., K, number of eigenvectors), and current selection heuristics are ad-hoc, often based on general data properties rather than the specific biological question. This leads to inconsistent downstream analyses, especially when identifying disease-relevant cell states or predicting treatment response, as the imputed signal may obscure or distort biologically critical variation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimal imputation parameters for a given analysis can be systematically selected by optimizing the performance of a supervised learning task (e.g., classifying disease state or treatment outcome) on the imputed data, thereby aligning the imputation directly with the scientific goal.
proposals.method: We will frame parameter selection as a model selection problem within a supervised learning pipeline. For a dataset with patient-level labels (e.g., disease/healthy, treatment responder/non-responder), we will perform nested cross-validation. In the inner loop, for candidate parameter values, we will impute the training fold, train a classifier (e.g., a sparse logistic regression model or an attention-based model as in 'Supervised learning pays attention'), and evaluate on the validation fold. The parameter maximizing validation AUC will be selected. The final model will be trained on the full training set with this optimal parameter. We will extend this core idea to the annotation-free cell discovery setting by using the stability of recovered disease-relevant cell clusters as the optimization target.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, automated, and goal-oriented framework for single-cell data imputation, moving beyond generic metrics. It will directly improve the reliability of downstream analyses for biomarker and rare cell population discovery. The method will be implemented as an open-source software package, integrating with popular single-cell analysis workflows.
proposals.novelty: While supervised tuning is common in machine learning, its application to optimize the fundamental data pre-processing step of imputation is novel. The approach uniquely leverages the patient and treatment labels already present in many study designs to guide imputation, creating a tight coupling between data cleaning and biological inference that current methods lack.

proposals.title: Sparse Regression for Scalable, Annotation-Free Imputation of Single-Cell Genomic Matrices
proposals.motivation: The scale of single-cell RNA-seq datasets is rapidly increasing, with studies now profiling millions of cells from thousands of patients. Efficiently handling missing data (dropouts) in these very large, sparse cell-by-gene matrices is a critical bottleneck for downstream analysis, including the identification of disease-relevant cell states and predictive modeling for patient treatment response. Current imputation methods struggle with computational efficiency and often rely on pre-defined cell type labels, which can introduce bias.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fast, univariate-guided sparse regression framework, applied in an annotation-free manner, can accurately impute missing gene expression values in massive genomic matrices by leveraging the intrinsic low-rank structure and gene-gene correlation patterns within the data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage algorithm. First, we will perform a univariate screening of genes to identify a subset of highly informative 'landmark' features for each target gene, drawing on the principles of sure independence screening. Second, we will fit a sparse regression model (e.g., lasso or elastic net) for each gene using only its selected landmarks as predictors. This regression will be trained in a self-supervised manner by artificially masking observed counts, avoiding the need for external cell annotations. Computational scalability will be achieved through block-wise coordinate descent and parallelization across genes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a computationally efficient, scalable tool for imputing ultra-large-scale single-cell datasets, directly enabling more powerful supervised learning tasks like predicting disease outcome or immune response from genomic data. It will also demonstrate how sparse regression, a cornerstone of Tibshirani's research, can be adapted for foundational, annotation-free data preprocessing in modern genomics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging the scalability of univariate screening with the precision of sparse regression specifically for the imputation task, while entirely removing the dependency on cell type labels that limits existing methods, aligning with recent trends in annotation-free discovery.

proposals.title: Nearest Shrunken Centroids with Stability Selection and Synthetic Supervision for Ultra-High-Dimensional, Low-Sample-Size Biological Data
proposals.motivation: In single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, researchers often aim to identify cell states or patient subtypes from datasets where the number of features (genes) vastly exceeds the number of samples (cells or patients). The Nearest Shrunken Centroids (NSC) classifier is a natural choice for such p>>n problems due to its built-in feature selection, but its performance degrades severely with extremely small n, leading to unstable and inaccurate centroids. This limits its application in critical areas like rare disease classification or minimal biopsy analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating stability selection for feature inclusion with a supervised contrastive learning objective to refine centroid estimation will significantly improve the robustness and classification accuracy of NSC in ultra-low-sample-size regimes.
proposals.method: We will extend NSC by first applying stability selection via subsampling to identify a robust, consensus set of features, reducing variance in the initial selection. Second, we will treat the shrunken class centroids as learnable parameters and optimize them using a supervised contrastive loss that pulls centroid representations of the same class together while pushing apart different classes, leveraging the training labels. This dual-stage approach—stability-based filtering followed by centroid refinement via a metric learning objective—will be implemented and tested on single-cell disease classification and patient tumor subtyping benchmarks with n < 20.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous and practical upgrade to a foundational p>>n classifier, making it viable for the smallest-sample biological studies. It will deliver a new open-source tool for reliable cell-type and disease-state discovery in data-scarce settings, directly impacting translational research.
proposals.novelty: While stability selection and contrastive learning exist separately, their integrated application to stabilize and *train* the centroids of a generative classifier like NSC is novel. This moves beyond simple regularization or ensemble methods by using the labels to directly supervise the centroid-shrinking process itself.

proposals.title: Sparse Genetic Signature Recovery via Compressive Sensing for Patient-Specific Tumor Profiling
proposals.motivation: Microarray and bulk RNA-seq data for cancer patients present a high-dimensional feature selection problem (tens of thousands of genes) with limited observations (dozens to hundreds of patients). Traditional penalized regression methods like the lasso are effective but treat the problem purely statistically, ignoring the underlying signal structure. Compressive sensing theory, which provides guarantees for recovering sparse signals from limited measurements, offers a principled framework to potentially improve the identification of robust, biologically interpretable genetic signatures predictive of disease state or treatment response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating microarray feature selection as a compressive sensing signal recovery problem will yield sparser, more stable, and more biologically coherent genetic signatures than standard lasso regression, particularly in small-sample, high-dimensional oncology datasets.
proposals.method: We will frame the patient-by-gene expression matrix as a set of linear measurements of a sparse signal (the disease-relevant genetic program). Using patient labels (e.g., disease subtype, treatment response), we will apply Basis Pursuit Denoising via L1-minimization solvers (e.g., SPGL1) under conditions promoting the Restricted Isometry Property. Feature selection stability will be assessed via subsampling, and identified gene sets will be validated for biological coherence using pathway enrichment analysis (e.g., Gene Ontology) and cross-referenced with cell-type-specific markers from single-cell atlases to link signatures to tumor or immune cell states.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodological bridge between compressive sensing theory and genomic feature selection, delivering a more robust tool for identifying diagnostic and prognostic biomarkers from limited patient cohorts. It will contribute a concrete, interpretable framework for distilling high-dimensional molecular data into sparse, actionable genetic signatures relevant to specific tumor or immune cell biology.
proposals.novelty: While Tibshirani's prior work on the lasso is foundational, directly applying the theoretical guarantees and recovery algorithms of compressive sensing to genetic feature selection is a non-obvious synthesis. The novelty lies in explicitly treating the genetic signature as a compressible signal to be recovered, rather than solely as coefficients to be penalized for prediction.

proposals.title: Bayesian Model Averaging for Robust Single-Cell Classification of Patient Immune Response
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, classifying cell states or predicting patient treatment response from high-dimensional gene expression data is challenging due to biological and technical noise. Current supervised methods, including sparse regression and attention-based models, often yield unstable feature sets and predictions when trained on different gene subsets or batches, hindering reproducible biomarker discovery for disease and immune profiling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Bayesian model averaging (BMA) over the vast space of possible predictive gene subsets will yield more stable and robust classifiers for cell-type and patient-level outcomes than any single sparse model.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will use a univariate-guided procedure (inspired by 'Univariate-Guided Sparse Regression') to generate a candidate set of high-priority gene features from single-cell RNA-seq data, annotated with cell labels (e.g., disease state). Second, we will employ Bayesian Model Averaging, using a spike-and-slab prior for feature inclusion, to average predictions across thousands of logistic regression models built on random subsets of these candidate genes. The model will be implemented using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) or variational inference for scalability.
