proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, uncertainty-quantified classification tool for single-cell and bulk genomic data, directly applicable to predicting patient treatment response or disease severity. It will advance the field by moving beyond single 'best' sparse models to an ensemble that accounts for feature selection uncertainty, improving reproducibility in translational research.
proposals.novelty: While BMA is established in statistics, its application to average over gene subsets for single-cell classification, guided by univariate screening to make the model space tractable, is novel. This bridges Tibshirani's work on sparse regression and stable feature selection with modern Bayesian computational tools for genomics.

proposals.title: Scalable Nearest Shrunken Centroids for High-Dimensional Genomic Profiling: Enabling Sparse, Interpretable Classification from Millions of Features
proposals.motivation: The transition from microarray to next-generation sequencing (NGS) data in genomics and single-cell biology has created a 'p >> n' problem of unprecedented scale, with millions of potential genomic features (e.g., SNPs, rare variants, open chromatin regions) per patient or cell. Existing sparse classification methods, including the original Nearest Shrunken Centroids (NSC), struggle computationally and statistically with this dimensionality, hindering the discovery of robust, interpretable biomarkers for disease subtypes, treatment response, and immune cell states from modern datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating a univariate feature screening step informed by outcome labels with a reformulated, block-coordinate optimization for the NSC loss, we can create a scalable algorithm that maintains NSC's interpretability and sparsity while efficiently handling millions of features.
proposals.method: We will adapt the NSC algorithm in three stages. First, we will employ a univariate-guided, data-adaptive screening step (inspired by 'Univariate-Guided Sparse Regression') to reduce the feature set from millions to a computationally manageable size (e.g., O(10^4)), preserving features with strong marginal associations to the training labels (e.g., disease status, treatment outcome). Second, we will reformulate the NSC optimization using a block-coordinate descent framework suitable for distributed computation. Finally, we will integrate this with a feature attention mechanism from supervised learning to weight screened features during centroid shrinkage, prioritizing the most disease-relevant genomic loci or cells in the final model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source tool for performing sparse, interpretable classification directly on ultra-high-dimensional genomic data without requiring pre-filtering to gene-level counts. It will enable researchers to identify concise sets of genomic features—from single-nucleotide variants to regulatory elements—predictive of clinical outcomes or defining novel cell states, directly linking NGS-scale measurements to biological and clinical interpretation.
proposals.novelty: While feature screening is common, its integration into the NSC framework specifically for NGS-scale data is novel, as is the proposed synthesis of univariate guidance for scalability with supervised attention mechanisms to preserve the biological interpretability of the shrunken centroids for cell- and patient-level analysis.

proposals.title: Inverse Inference in Precision Oncology: A Sparse, Interpretable Model for Predicting Prior Treatment from Single-Cell Transcriptomic Signatures
proposals.motivation: In clinical oncology and translational immunology, patient biopsies often present with complex molecular signatures whose origins are unclear. A critical, unsolved problem is determining whether an observed signature reflects a specific prior treatment (e.g., immune checkpoint blockade) or the natural history of the disease state. Solving this inverse problem would enhance the interpretation of post-treatment biopsies, aid in identifying treatment-resistant clones, and inform subsequent therapeutic decisions, all within the pre-2026 paradigm of precision medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparse, supervised model can be trained on single-cell RNA-seq data from annotated patient cohorts to accurately and interpretably predict a patient's prior treatment or disease subtype based solely on the transcriptional state of key cell populations, particularly tumor and immune cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage large, publicly available single-cell datasets (e.g., from tumor atlases) with known treatment labels. Building on Tibshirani's recent work on annotation-free cell discovery and univariate-guided sparse regression, we will first identify disease- and treatment-relevant cell subpopulations without manual annotation. We will then engineer patient-level features summarizing these cell states. A sparse multinomial regression model (e.g., lasso or elastic net) will be trained to predict the treatment/disease class from these features, ensuring interpretability by selecting a minimal set of predictive cellular signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational tool for the retrospective analysis of treatment effects in complex tissues. It will contribute a statistically rigorous, interpretable framework for the inverse problem in molecular pathology, moving beyond descriptive signatures to predictive, causal-inference-ready models. The method will directly aid in deciphering the molecular legacy of therapies in clinical samples.
proposals.novelty: While most supervised learning in genomics predicts outcome from treatment, we invert this to predict treatment from outcome (the molecular signature). This approach uniquely combines Tibshirani's signature sparse modeling for interpretability with the emerging capability to define features from annotation-free, disease-relevant cell states in single-cell data.

proposals.title: Correlation-Aware Sparse Regression for Robust Single-Cell Biomarker Discovery
proposals.motivation: In high-dimensional biological data, such as single-cell RNA sequencing, predictor variables (e.g., gene expression) are highly correlated due to co-regulation and biological pathways. The standard Lasso, which assumes near-independence, performs poorly under such conditions, selecting variables arbitrarily from correlated groups and yielding unstable, uninterpretable models. This is a critical barrier to reliably identifying disease-relevant cell states or predictive gene signatures for patient stratification and treatment response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the correlation structure of predictors—particularly the block-correlation patterns inherent in genomic data—within a sparse regression framework will yield more stable, biologically interpretable, and predictive models of clinical outcomes like disease severity or immune treatment response.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method building on pre-2026 concepts. First, we will use an unsupervised graph-based clustering (e.g., on the gene correlation network) to identify blocks of correlated features. Second, we will implement a sparse group Lasso or cluster-guided penalty that encourages selection of entire correlated blocks or representative features from them, integrating this with the supervised learning framework from 'Supervised learning pays attention'. Model performance will be evaluated on simulated data with known correlation structures and on real single-cell datasets with patient-level labels (e.g., tumor vs. normal, treatment responder vs. non-responder).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, statistically rigorous method for sparse regression in the presence of high correlation, directly applicable to modern single-cell genomics. It will advance the goal of 'Annotation-free discovery of disease-relevant cells' by producing more robust and replicable biomarkers. The method will be disseminated as open-source software, enabling more reliable translation of high-dimensional biological data into clinical insights.
proposals.novelty: While the instability of Lasso under correlation is known, our approach is novel in its direct integration of unsupervised correlation structure discovery with supervised, outcome-guided sparse regression, specifically tailored to the block-correlation patterns and clinical prediction tasks prevalent in single-cell biology.

proposals.title: A Quadratic Programming Framework for the Lasso: Efficient Solutions for High-Dimensional Single-Cell Feature Selection
proposals.motivation: The lasso is a cornerstone for feature selection in high-dimensional genomic and single-cell data, crucial for identifying patient- or disease-relevant cell populations. However, its optimization, typically solved via coordinate descent, can be inefficient for very large-scale problems with complex correlation structures. Leveraging mature quadratic programming (QP) literature could provide more stable, interpretable, and computationally efficient solutions, directly benefiting modern supervised analyses of tumor and immune cell datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reformulating the lasso as a quadratic program with linear inequality constraints will yield novel, efficient optimization algorithms that outperform standard coordinate descent in specific high-dimensional regimes, particularly for problems with structured covariates or when integrated with path-following strategies.
proposals.method: We will reformulate the standard lasso objective into a canonical QP form by introducing auxiliary variables to handle the absolute values in the L1 penalty, resulting in a problem with linear constraints. We will implement and benchmark specialized active-set and interior-point QP solvers against the glmnet coordinate descent algorithm. The framework will be tested on synthetic data and real single-cell RNA-seq datasets, where features are genes and the task is supervised prediction of disease state or treatment response from cell-type abundances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, principled optimization perspective on the lasso, potentially leading to faster convergence for certain problem classes. It will deliver open-source software integrating QP solvers into the high-dimensional analysis toolkit, enabling more robust feature selection in biomedical applications like annotating disease-relevant cells from single-cell data without explicit labels.
proposals.novelty: While the lasso's QP representation is known in theory, a deep, practical investigation and benchmarking of modern QP algorithms for this specific problem, especially in the context of contemporary single-cell data analysis, is lacking. This bridges a gap between classical optimization literature and current needs in statistical machine learning for biology.

proposals.title: Sparse, Interpretable Clustering of Single-Cell and Bulk Expression Data for the Discovery of Prognostic DLBCL Subtypes
proposals.motivation: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL) exhibits significant clinical heterogeneity, with current cell-of-origin classifications (GCB/ABC) failing to fully explain treatment response variability. The advent of high-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and large-scale bulk expression cohorts provides an unprecedented opportunity to discover novel, biologically coherent subtypes. Identifying such subtypes is critical for developing more precise prognostic models and tailoring immune-based therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying sparse, feature-selective clustering algorithms to integrated single-cell and bulk tumor expression data will reveal novel, biologically interpretable DLBCL subgroups with distinct clinical outcomes and immune microenvironments.
proposals.method: We will integrate bulk RNA-seq from cohorts like the Lymphoma/Leukemia Molecular Profiling Project with scRNA-seq from DLBCL tumors and healthy lymphoid tissue. Initial unsupervised discovery will employ sparse K-means and convex clustering, guided by univariate feature selection to prioritize genes with high variance and prognostic value. Discovered clusters will be validated for stability and then characterized using supervised learning (e.g., LASSO-regularized Cox models) to predict patient survival and treatment response, linking cluster labels to immune cell composition and pathway activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, data-driven molecular taxonomy of DLBCL that moves beyond the GCB/ABC dichotomy. It will provide clinicians with a sparse gene signature for subtype assignment and reveal subtype-specific immune contexts, potentially identifying patient subgroups more likely to benefit from specific immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely integrates sparse clustering principles from Tibshirani's prior work with the annotation-free cell discovery framework from recent papers, applying them to the specific challenge of DLBCL subtyping by jointly modeling bulk patient outcomes and single-cell resolution.

proposals.title: Sparse-Adaptive Dispersion Shrinkage for Feature Selection in Single-Cell and Multi-Omic Disease Profiling
proposals.motivation: Single-cell and spatial omics technologies generate extremely high-dimensional, sparse feature matrices (e.g., gene expression, protein abundance) where most measurements are zero. Traditional feature selection methods, like variance-based filtering or lasso regression, can be misled by this sparsity, failing to distinguish biologically meaningful signal from technical noise. This is critical for identifying disease-relevant cell states and predictive biomarkers from patient samples to guide immune and treatment strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the dispersion measure and its reference distribution to explicitly model the zero-inflated nature of sparse biological data will yield more stable and biologically interpretable feature selection, improving downstream supervised learning for disease classification and patient stratification.
proposals.method: We will develop a sparse-adaptive dispersion statistic that down-weights the influence of excess zeros, integrating it with a tailored reference distribution generated via permutation that preserves the empirical zero structure. This forms a 'Sparse-Adaptive Dispersion Score' (SADS). We will embed SADS within a univariate-guided sparse regression framework (extending Tibshirani's recent work) to pre-screen features before applying a group-lasso-type penalty, ensuring selection stability. The method will be validated on annotated and annotation-free single-cell disease datasets (e.g., from tumor microenvironments).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically principled feature selection tool specifically designed for the zero-inflated, high-dimensional data ubiquitous in modern biology. It will enhance the discovery of robust, disease-relevant cellular features from patient samples without requiring prior annotation, directly contributing to more accurate predictive models for treatment response. The software implementation will extend the scikit-learn and R ecosystems for widespread use.
proposals.novelty: While dispersion-based filtering exists (e.g., in scRNA-seq pipelines), a formal statistical recalibration of dispersion for feature selection in supervised learning, coupled with a reference distribution respecting sparsity, is novel. This bridges univariate filtering with regularized regression in a way specifically optimized for the sparse data structures common in post-genomic biology.

proposals.title: Sparse, Interpretable Non-Linear Models for Single-Cell Phenotype Prediction
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, the relationship between gene expression and clinical outcomes like disease state or treatment response is often complex and non-linear. While the lasso provides interpretable, sparse models for linear relationships, its application is limited in this high-dimensional, non-linear setting. Extending lasso-style regularization to non-linear models would enable the discovery of biologically interpretable, predictive features from complex cellular data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying structured sparsity constraints, akin to the lasso, to additive or kernel-based regression models will yield more accurate and interpretable predictors of patient-level phenotypes from single-cell data than linear models, while identifying key cell states and genes driving the predictions.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method building on pre-2026 foundations. First, we will use recent annotation-free techniques (e.g., from 'Annotation-free discovery...') to distill single-cell profiles into candidate cellular 'features'. Second, we will apply a sparse additive model (SpAM) or a kernelized lasso (KLASSO) with an L1 penalty on the non-linear component coefficients to predict patient labels (e.g., disease severity, immune response). Model training will use coordinate descent, with attention mechanisms to highlight influential cells and genes, as inspired by 'Supervised learning pays attention'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, sparse modeling framework for non-linear relationships in high-dimensional biomedical data, directly addressing the limitations of linear assumptions in genomics. It will contribute a method for identifying specific cell subpopulations and their non-linear gene expression patterns that are predictive of clinical outcomes, enhancing biological discovery and potential biomarker identification.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of lasso-driven sparsity into flexible non-linear prediction models specifically for the structure of single-cell data, moving beyond the linear Generalized Additive Model (GAM) lasso extensions to a framework that directly incorporates modern, unsupervised cellular feature discovery.

proposals.title: Sparse Regression with Correlated Errors: A Generalized Lasso for Single-Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics
proposals.motivation: The lasso and its variants assume independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) errors, an assumption frequently violated in modern biomedical data. In single-cell RNA-seq, cells from the same patient or spatial transcriptomics spots exhibit autocorrelation due to shared biology and technical batch effects. Ignoring this structure leads to invalid standard errors, inflated false discovery rates, and unreliable feature selection for identifying disease-relevant cells or predictive biomarkers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling the error covariance structure within a penalized regression framework will yield more accurate and interpretable sparse models for correlated data, improving the identification of biologically and clinically relevant features in single-cell and spatial genomics.
proposals.method: We will develop a Generalized Lasso (G-Lasso) estimator that minimizes a penalized generalized least squares (GLS) objective: β̂ = argmin_β (y - Xβ)^T Σ^{-1}(y - Xβ) + λ||β||_1, where Σ models the error covariance. For single-cell data, Σ will be structured using patient-of-origin or phenotypic labels as random effects (e.g., a block-diagonal form). For spatial or time-series data, we will employ autoregressive or Gaussian Process kernels. Estimation will use an iterative algorithm alternating between updating Σ (via residual moments or restricted maximum likelihood) and solving the weighted lasso, with extensions to the elastic net for grouped features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous, practical tool for sparse regression in the presence of correlated errors, directly applicable to single-cell and spatial omics. It will improve the robustness of feature selection for identifying cell states associated with disease, immune response, or treatment outcome. The method will be implemented in an open-source R package, extending the `glmnet` ecosystem.
proposals.novelty: While generalized least squares and the lasso are well-established, their integration for structured high-dimensional data, particularly with a focus on experimental designs common in single-cell biology (e.g., multi-patient cohorts with nested cells), is not fully addressed. This proposal directly bridges Tibshirani's foundational work on sparse modeling with the specific correlated error structures pervasive in post-2020 genomic technologies.

proposals.title: Homotopy-Enhanced Sparse Regression for Interpretable Single-Cell Biomarker Discovery
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying sparse, interpretable gene signatures that distinguish cell states or predict patient outcomes is critical for understanding disease mechanisms and treatment response. The Lasso is a cornerstone for this, but standard solvers provide a single model for a fixed regularization parameter, obscuring the stability and order in which features enter the solution path. A deeper analysis of this path can reveal robust, hierarchical biological signals.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying advanced homotopy continuation methods to trace the exact Lasso solution path across all regularization strengths will yield more stable and interpretable feature sets for single-cell and patient-level prediction, revealing a hierarchy of disease-relevant cellular biomarkers.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the LARS (Least Angle Regression) homotopy algorithm to high-dimensional single-cell and bulk genomic datasets, integrating it with supervised learning frameworks for disease classification. The core innovation is to use the resulting piecewise-linear solution paths not just for model selection, but as a primary analytical object. We will develop metrics to quantify feature path stability and use this to guide the selection of robust gene or cell-type signatures predictive of immune response or treatment outcome, moving towards annotation-free discovery of critical cellular players.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool that delivers not just a sparse model, but a complete stability ranking of features, enhancing biological interpretability. It will bridge applied mathematics (homotopy methods) with translational bioinformatics, offering a principled alternative to cross-validation for high-dimensional biomarker discovery in oncology and immunology.
proposals.novelty: While LARS is known, its systematic application as a diagnostic and discovery tool for single-cell data, particularly for deriving stable, hierarchical biological interpretations from supervised learning tasks, is novel and directly extends Tibshirani's recent work on univariate guidance and annotation-free cell discovery.

proposals.title: Bayesian Model Averaging with Lasso Priors for Robust, Interpretable Feature Selection in Single-Cell Disease Profiling
proposals.motivation: In single-cell genomics, identifying cell states and molecular features predictive of patient disease, immune response, or treatment outcome is critical. Current methods, including the lasso, provide point estimates but lack principled uncertainty quantification, which is essential for robust biomarker discovery. A Bayesian framework that explicitly encodes sparsity can combine the interpretability of feature selection with the probabilistic rigor of model averaging.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the lasso constraint via appropriate continuous shrinkage priors within a Bayesian model averaging (BMA) framework will yield more stable and probabilistically interpretable selections of disease-relevant cells and genes than standard penalized regression, especially when integrating data from multiple patients or treatment conditions.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian model where coefficients for cell-type or gene expression features follow a continuous prior that induces sparsity, such as the horseshoe or a Laplace-scale mixture, directly translating the lasso's L1 penalty. We will employ variational inference or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for scalable posterior computation. The method will be applied to annotated single-cell datasets (with disease/treatment labels) to perform supervised discovery, with model averaging used to aggregate over uncertainty in the selected feature set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a fully Bayesian analog to the lasso for high-dimensional biological data, delivering calibrated posterior inclusion probabilities for features like cell types. It will contribute a new tool for 'annotation-free' discovery by probabilistically identifying cell populations associated with clinical labels, directly advancing the integration of supervised learning with interpretable, sparse models in computational biology.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian analogs to the lasso exist, their integration into a formal model averaging framework for the specific task of supervised single-cell analysis—bridging Tibshirani's foundational work on sparsity with modern Bayesian variable selection—is novel. This moves beyond point estimation to provide a unified probabilistic output for feature importance.

proposals.title: Cross-Validated Sparse Feature Selection for Single-Cell Phenotype Discovery in Cancer Immunology
proposals.motivation: In single-cell RNA-seq analysis of tumor and immune cells, identifying a minimal, interpretable set of gene features predictive of patient outcome or treatment response is critical. The lasso is a natural tool for this, but its performance hinges on the choice of the tuning parameter t. Current heuristic or information-criterion-based selection methods, common in genomics, may not be optimized for predictive accuracy and stability in complex, high-dimensional biological datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing rigorous k-fold cross-validation (CV) to select the lasso's tuning parameter will yield more robust and biologically interpretable gene signatures for cell-state and disease-relevant classification than standard selection rules, leading to improved generalization to unseen patient data.
proposals.method: We will apply the lasso to single-cell datasets with patient-level labels (e.g., disease state, treatment response). For each candidate regularization parameter t, we will perform k-fold cross-validation, partitioning data at the patient level to avoid leakage. The model's predictive performance (e.g., AUC for classification, MSE for regression) will be averaged across folds. The t value yielding the optimal CV score will be selected. This CV-lasso framework will be benchmarked against fixed-rule selections (e.g., one-standard-error-rule, BIC) on held-out test sets and validated via biological pathway analysis of the selected features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, prediction-focused framework for sparse feature selection in single-cell immunogenomics, directly linking statistical regularization to clinical predictive utility. It will deliver a reproducible pipeline and practical guidelines for tuning parameter selection in biomedical applications of the lasso, moving beyond purely descriptive feature lists.
proposals.novelty: While CV is standard in machine learning, its systematic application and benchmarking for tuning the lasso in the context of single-cell data with patient-level outcomes, particularly for annotation-free discovery of disease-relevant cells, is not yet a settled practice. This bridges a gap between rigorous ML methodology and pressing biomedical analysis needs.

proposals.title: Inverting the Lasso: Recovering the Regularization Constraint from a Sparse Coefficient Vector
proposals.motivation: In biomedical applications like single-cell RNA-seq analysis, researchers often interpret sparse models (e.g., identifying disease-relevant cells or features) after fitting a lasso. However, the exact regularization strength (bound t) used is frequently lost or unreported, hindering reproducibility and precise understanding of model selection. Recovering t from a given coefficient vector is an unsolved inverse problem critical for auditing, sharing, and comparing sparse models in patient studies.
proposals.hypothesis: For a given design matrix X and response y, the lasso bound t that produced a specific sparse solution β̂ can be uniquely recovered or accurately estimated by leveraging the optimality conditions of the lasso problem.
proposals.method: We will formalize the problem using the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions for the lasso. For a known β̂, the minimal feasible t is t_min = ||X^T(y - Xβ̂)||_∞. We will develop algorithms to compute this exactly and to estimate t when only β̂ and the active set are known, but X and y are not fully available (e.g., in published summaries). This involves solving a constrained optimization problem to find a compatible X and y, inspired by inverse covariance estimation methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal method and software tool to recover the lasso tuning parameter from any sparse solution, enhancing the interpretability and reproducibility of sparse models in genomics and clinical prediction. It will enable better meta-analysis of published models and facilitate the 'annotation' of discovered cell types or features with their precise selection constraints.
proposals.novelty: While vast literature focuses on solving the lasso for a given t, the inverse problem—finding t from β̂—is largely unaddressed. This directly inverts the standard paradigm, connecting a model's solution back to its generative constraint, which is novel for both statistical theory and applied data science.

proposals.title: Streaming Sparse Regression for Real-Time Cellular Phenotyping
proposals.motivation: In clinical single-cell analysis, such as monitoring immune response during treatment or detecting rare tumor cells, there is a growing need for real-time, on-device inference. Current sparse regression methods like the lasso are too computationally intensive for streaming data or resource-constrained embedded systems in point-of-care diagnostics. This limits the translation of supervised cell-state classifiers to dynamic, time-sensitive applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a univariate-guided, approximate lasso algorithm, using a single-pass coordinate descent with online thresholding, can achieve near-original predictive accuracy with orders-of-magnitude speedup, enabling real-time cell-type calling from streaming cytometers.
proposals.method: We will develop an 'Online Univariate-Guisted Lasso' (OUGL). The method first calculates univariate feature importance statistics (e.g., correlation with the disease label) from an initial batch to order and screen variables. For streaming data, we employ a single-pass, cyclic coordinate descent update restricted to this active set, using a proximal gradient step with an adaptive, decaying regularization parameter. Approximation is controlled via a KKT-condition check tolerance, allowing trade-offs between speed and exact sparsity. We will validate on pre-2026 single-cell RNA-seq datasets with patient treatment response labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously tested, ultra-fast sparse regression tool tailored for biomedical streaming data. It will extend the lasso's interpretability to real-time applications, such as instant annotation of disease-relevant cells in flow cytometry data during surgery or continuous patient monitoring. The algorithm will be released as a lightweight, open-source software package.
proposals.novelty: While approximate lasso solvers exist, none are designed for the single-pass, memory-limited context of streaming single-cell data with a focus on preserving the univariate-guided feature selection insight from Tibshirani's recent work, ensuring biological interpretability is not sacrificed for speed.

proposals.title: Adversarial Predictive Validation of a Sensorimotor Network Model for Multiple Sclerosis Progression
proposals.motivation: In multiple sclerosis (MS), predicting individual patient disease trajectories remains a major clinical challenge. While descriptive models link lesion location to symptoms, they often fail to provide robust, prospective, and mechanistically grounded predictions of future decline. A competitive, prospective validation framework is needed to move from correlative associations to causally interpretable models with genuine clinical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: A mechanistic sensorimotor control model, defined by baseline structural connectivity disruptions from lesions, will prospectively and significantly outperform non-mechanistic benchmark models (e.g., based on total lesion volume or simple regional counts) in predicting the rate of motor and sensory decline in a longitudinal MS cohort.
proposals.method: We will implement a pre-registered, adversarial prediction study. Baseline multi-modal MRI from a large, diverse MS cohort will be used to compute features for competing models: 1) a mechanistic model using graph-theoretic measures of sensorimotor network disruption, and 2) benchmark models using conventional radiomic and clinical features. Using supervised learning with sparse regression techniques (e.g., the lasso or elastic net) for regularization, we will train models to predict a composite measure of future clinical decline. Performance will be rigorously evaluated via nested cross-validation, comparing out-of-sample prediction error (RMSE) and using Bayesian model comparison to assess explanatory power.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, prospective test of a mechanistic model in MS, establishing a template for adversarial validation in neurological disease. It will directly inform patient stratification by identifying which baseline imaging features are most predictive, potentially guiding targeted treatment strategies. The successful model would offer a more interpretable, biologically grounded tool for prognosis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in a new model per se, but in the adversarial, pre-registered framework that competitively tests a mechanistic hypothesis against simpler alternatives using prospective prediction as the arbiter, moving beyond post-hoc correlation. This approach integrates Tibshirani's focus on sparse, interpretable prediction with a causal inference-inspired validation paradigm.

proposals.title: Robust Post-Selection Inference for High-Dimensional Models with Non-Gaussian Errors
proposals.motivation: Selective inference methods, crucial for valid inference after model selection (e.g., the lasso), predominantly rely on Gaussian error assumptions. However, real-world biomedical data, such as single-cell RNA-seq counts or patient treatment response measures, often exhibit heavy tails, outliers, or discrete distributions. This mismatch can invalidate p-values and confidence intervals, misleading scientific conclusions in disease studies where identifying relevant cells or patient features is key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by leveraging robust M-estimation theory and data-splitting strategies, we can construct post-selection p-values and confidence intervals that maintain asymptotic validity and finite-sample reliability under a broad class of error distributions, including heavy-tailed and contaminated models.
proposals.method: We will extend the selective inference framework by developing a two-stage procedure. First, we will employ a robust penalized regression method (e.g., using a Huber loss or L1 loss for the likelihood) for the initial model selection step. Second, for inference on the selected features, we will derive asymptotic distributions for the robust estimators conditional on selection, potentially using a sample-splitting approach to decouple selection and inference. We will validate the method's Type I error control and power via simulations with t-distributed and contaminated Gaussian errors, and apply it to a single-cell dataset to identify disease-relevant immune cell populations with reliable confidence statements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general-purpose selective inference toolkit that relaxes the Gaussian error requirement, enabling reliable statistical inference in high-dimensional biology and medicine where non-normality is common. It will directly enhance the reproducibility of findings from supervised learning pipelines that pay attention to specific cells or patient features.
proposals.novelty: While existing robust regression methods ignore selection bias, and selective inference methods assume normality, our proposal uniquely integrates these two lines of work to address a critical gap in the analysis of modern, messy biological data.

proposals.title: Designing Single-Cell Sequencing Studies for Optimal Feature Selection in Cancer Immunology
proposals.motivation: In single-cell cancer immunology, data collection (e.g., patient cohort selection, cell sampling) is often ad-hoc, driven by convenience rather than statistical principles. This can lead to biased feature selection, where discovered cell states or gene signatures are artifacts of the sampling scheme, compromising their validity for predicting treatment response or disease outcome. A principled framework to design data collection for robust inference is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By formally inverting the feature selection problem, we can design optimal patient and cell sampling strategies that guarantee, with high probability, the recovery of sparse, biologically interpretable models for immune-tumor interactions that generalize to new cohorts.
proposals.method: We will frame study design as an optimization over the space of potential cohorts and cell-type proportions. Building on the Lasso and the 'smoothly clipped absolute deviation' (SCAD) penalty, we will derive conditions for consistent variable selection as a function of the design matrix. Using pre-2026 single-cell atlases (e.g., from tumor microenvironments) as a prior distribution of cellular features, we will develop a computational algorithm to solve for the minimal-cost sample collection plan (e.g., which patients to sequence, how many cells to profile per patient) that meets these statistical conditions. The method will integrate supervised learning objectives (e.g., predicting immune therapy labels) with annotation-free cell discovery to guide design.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, optimization-based tool for planning single-cell studies, moving beyond power calculations for simple tests to ensuring robust discovery of complex, sparse biological mechanisms. It will yield concrete guidelines for resource allocation in translational immunology and a new statistical paradigm of 'design for selection' applicable beyond genomics.
proposals.novelty: While existing work focuses on correcting selection bias post-hoc or on experimental design for mean estimation, this proposal inverts the causal pathway: it starts with the desired inferential property (sparse model recovery) and designs the data collection procedure to achieve it, a novel synthesis of experimental design and high-dimensional inference.

proposals.title: A Rigorous Evaluation of Hierarchical Clustering for Robust and Clinically Actionable Patient Subtyping in Single-Cell Oncology
proposals.motivation: Unsupervised subtyping of patients based on high-dimensional molecular data, such as single-cell RNA-seq from tumors, is crucial for precision oncology. However, the robustness and clinical relevance of subtypes discovered by popular methods like k-means or non-negative matrix factorization are often unstable and difficult to interpret biologically. A systematic, empirical comparison grounded in clinical outcomes is needed to guide method selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hierarchical clustering, due to its deterministic nature and ability to produce a nested tree structure, yields more robust and clinically interpretable patient subtypes than partition-based or latent factor methods when applied to single-cell-derived patient features.
proposals.method: We will leverage large, annotated single-cell datasets (e.g., from tumor atlases) to create patient-level feature matrices summarizing cell-type proportions, pathway activities, or key gene modules, informed by recent work on annotation-free cell discovery. We will compare hierarchical clustering (with various linkage and distance metrics) against k-means, spectral clustering, and NMF. Robustness will be assessed via bootstrapping and data perturbation, while clinical relevance will be evaluated by testing associations between subtypes and treatment response, survival, or immune infiltration labels using supervised learning frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven guideline for optimal subtyping in translational research, moving beyond algorithmic convenience. It will produce a formal evaluation framework for subtype robustness and deliver software to implement the recommended hierarchical clustering pipeline for clinical cohort analysis.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical clustering is widely used, its systematic defense as the *optimal* choice for clinically-relevant discovery is novel. The evaluation integrates modern single-cell feature engineering with a direct test of clinical utility, bridging unsupervised learning and supervised outcome analysis in a way not previously formalized.

proposals.title: Supervised and Feature-Guided Estimation of the Null Proportion in High-Throughput Genomic Studies
proposals.motivation: Accurate estimation of the proportion of true null hypotheses (pi0) is critical for controlling false discovery rates (FDR) in high-dimensional genomic studies, such as differential expression analysis in single-cell or bulk tumor data. Existing methods, like those from Storey (2002) or robust implementations in `qvalue`, can be sensitive to the shape of the p-value distribution and the presence of dense, weak signals, which are common in patient-level omics data. An improved pi0 estimator would directly enhance the reliability of biomarker and disease-relevant cell discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that leveraging supervised learning on auxiliary features (e.g., gene-level covariates) and guidance from univariate effect sizes can yield more robust and accurate pi0 estimates than methods relying solely on the p-value histogram, particularly in datasets with complex, structured alternatives.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that integrates Tibshirani's recent work on feature-guided inference. First, we will adapt the 'Univariate-Guided Sparse Regression' concept to model the local false discovery rate, using univariate test statistics as a guiding covariate. Second, we will employ a supervised learning approach, treating the null/alternative status as a latent label, and train a model (e.g., gradient boosting) on pre-2026 cell-level or gene-level features (e.g., mean expression, variance, network connectivity) to predict the probability of being null, which can be aggregated to estimate pi0. We will benchmark against standard methods (Storey, BUM) using simulated data and real single-cell RNA-seq datasets from tumor and immune cell studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more statistically powerful and stable pi0 estimator for modern biological studies, leading to improved FDR control in feature selection. It will bridge the gap between generic multiple testing corrections and the structured, feature-rich nature of genomic data, directly benefiting the analysis pipelines for identifying treatment-relevant cells and patient subtypes.
proposals.novelty: While pi0 estimation is a classic problem, the novel integration of supervised learning on biological features and univariate-guidance for this specific task is unexplored. This moves beyond histogram-based smoothing by incorporating domain-specific knowledge from the data generation process itself.

ideas.title: Bayesian Selective Inference for Data-Adaptive Procedures
ideas.description: This research direction develops a methodology for valid inference after a full data-adaptive modeling pipeline, such as tuning a lasso penalty via cross-validation. It integrates selective inference to condition on the model and hyperparameter selection event, while employing Bayesian model averaging over the posterior distribution of candidate models given that selection. This yields a posterior distribution that honestly reflects both the uncertainty in model form and the selection bias, providing calibrated credible intervals and model probabilities that account for the adaptive fitting process.

ideas.title: Post-Selection Inference with Correlated Features via Geophysical Signal Processing Methods
ideas.description: This idea addresses the challenge of performing valid inference after feature selection when the features are correlated. Drawing an analogy from geophysical signal processing, where correlated sensor noise is decorrelated using transforms like the Karhunen–Loève transform, we propose a method for selection-adjusted inference. The core adaptation is to estimate the feature correlation matrix from the data, compute its eigen-decomposition, and use this to define a whitening transform. The model selection procedure (e.g., Lasso, stepwise regression) and the subsequent hypothesis tests are formulated in this transformed space where the effective features are approximately orthogonal. This allows the application of existing post-selection inference methods that assume feature independence. Finally, the inference results (p-values, confidence intervals) are transformed back to the original, interpretable feature space, providing valid inference that accounts for the original correlation structure during the selection process.

ideas.title: Correlation-Robust, Data-Split Selection Inference with Decoupled Dependency Estimation
ideas.description: This method provides valid post-selection inference in the presence of unknown feature correlations by rigorously separating correlation estimation from the selection event. It employs a multi-split or resampling scheme where one subset of the data is used to estimate the feature covariance matrix without selection bias. This estimate then informs a constrained model selection procedure on independent data. Finally, valid p-values and confidence intervals are constructed using a conditional testing framework that accounts for the randomness in both the initial correlation estimation and the subsequent selection, ensuring robustness even when the true correlation structure is complex or high-dimensional.

ideas.title: Selective Inference via Astrophysical Signal Decomposition
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from radio astronomy to scale selective inference for ultra-high-dimensional data (p >> n). The analogous problem in astrophysics involves isolating discrete signals from a vast number of potential sources using data from a limited array of telescopes, solved by algorithms like CLEAN. The adapted method proposes an iterative selective inference procedure: in each cycle, apply a high-dimensional screening method (like marginal correlation or lasso) to the current residual data matrix to identify the most promising feature, conduct valid post-selection inference on that feature conditioned on the selection event, then orthogonalize the entire dataset with respect to the selected feature to create a new residual. This process is repeated, controlling for selection at each step, to build a sequentially valid set of inferences for discovered features, thereby managing the extreme dimensionality through iterative decomposition and residualization.

ideas.title: Post-Selection Inference via Bootstrap-Integrated Stability Paths
ideas.description: This method develops a resampling framework that explicitly accounts for the stochasticity of the model selection step itself. It employs a nested bootstrap or stability selection algorithm to generate a distribution of selected models and their associated parameter estimates from perturbed datasets. This allows for the direct estimation of selection probabilities, stability paths for parameters, and the construction of confidence intervals that are valid post-selection. The approach provides robust inference by quantifying the uncertainty introduced by the data-driven model choice, moving beyond naive bootstrapping of a single selected model.

ideas.title: A Unified Framework for Post-Selection Inference in Adaptive, Non-Linear Model Selection
ideas.description: This research develops a unified selective inference framework for complex, adaptive model-building workflows that integrate modern non-linear learners (e.g., trees, neural networks) with broad model selection procedures (e.g., genetic algorithms, stability selection, or reinforcement learning-based search). It moves beyond validating a single selected model to providing valid confidence intervals and p-values for features identified through an entire pipeline where the selection mechanism and the final model form are jointly data-adaptive. This enables statistically rigorous science in domains like genomics or automated machine learning (AutoML), where the analyst uses sophisticated algorithms to both search the model space and fit highly flexible, non-parametric functions.

ideas.title: Model-Agnostic Selective Conformal Inference for Adaptive Prediction
ideas.description: This research develops a robust inference framework for use after complex model selection by decoupling the selection and inference phases with data splitting and leveraging conformal prediction. The system treats the output of any black-box selection procedure (e.g., a chosen tree depth or neural network architecture) as a fixed pre-trained function. It then applies a conformal calibration step on held-out data to generate prediction intervals that maintain valid marginal coverage, and, through careful conditioning on the held-out set, approximate conditional validity given the selection. This approach provides practical, finite-sample guarantees for predictions from non-linear models without needing to mathematically characterize the selection event, making post-selection inference feasible for modern machine learning workflows.

ideas.title: Robust Selective Inference via Influence Functions and Resampling
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from robust statistics and econometrics, where influence functions and resampling methods are used to handle outliers and non-parametric error distributions. The analogous solution involves characterizing estimator robustness via influence functions and employing bootstrap techniques for inference without Gaussian assumptions. Adapted to selective inference, the new method will derive the asymptotic behavior of post-selection estimators using influence functions to account for the selection event, then employ a tailored, model-robostic bootstrap (e.g., a weighted or wild bootstrap) to compute valid selective p-values and confidence intervals. This approach relaxes the Gaussian error assumption, enabling reliable selective inference for data with heavy-tailed, skewed, or otherwise non-standard error distributions encountered in fields like quantitative finance, genomics, and observational studies.

ideas.title: Apply Multiple Testing Corrections from Astrophysics for Post-Selection Inference
ideas.description: The original idea proposes transferring FDR control methods from genomics for post-selection hypothesis testing. By analogy, the field of astrophysics faces a highly similar problem: after scanning vast datasets (e.g., for exoplanet transits or gamma-ray bursts), researchers must perform statistical inference on candidate signals while rigorously controlling for the fact they searched everywhere first—the 'look-elsewhere effect'. Their solution involves specialized multiple testing corrections, including FDR and FWER controls tailored for correlated, high-dimensional searches. This research adapts that astrophysical solution to the context of post-selection inference in statistics and machine learning. Specifically, after a data-driven model selection step from a large set of potential variables or models, we apply and potentially extend the correction procedures used in cosmology to the resulting parameter estimates. This yields statistically valid inference that accounts for the initial selection, directly parallel to how astrophysicists validate discoveries from sky surveys.

ideas.title: Co-designed Distributed Algorithms for Algorithmically-Aware Selective Inference
ideas.description: This research develops a new class of distributed computing algorithms where the statistical validity of post-selection inference is a first-order design constraint, not an afterthought. It formally models how distribution of data and computation (e.g., via consensus ADMM, distributed gradient methods, or federated learning rounds) creates new conditional selection events. The system then produces confidence intervals that are adjusted for both the initial data-driven selection and the subsequent algorithmic-path dependencies, ensuring robustness against the distortions introduced by the very parallelization meant to enable the calculation. This results in provably valid intervals for large-scale model selection, even when the inference is carried out on a decentralized computing fabric.

ideas.title: Improving computational efficiency of selective inference via precomputed lattice reduction and caching, inspired by crystallography
ideas.description: This idea aims to develop faster algorithms for computing p-values and confidence intervals after model selection by drawing an analogy from crystallography. In crystallography, the computationally intensive task of determining crystal structure from diffraction patterns was accelerated by precomputing 'structure factors' for canonical lattice symmetries and then applying symmetry operations to solve specific cases. We adapt this solution to selective inference by precomputing and caching the complex distributions or integration constants for a library of canonical selection events (e.g., the polyhedral regions corresponding to common lasso or stepwise selection outcomes). When performing inference for a new dataset, the algorithm will first map the observed selection event via identifiable symmetries (like sign and permutation groups) to a precomputed canonical form. The corresponding cached result is then transformed back, bypassing the need for a full, de novo numerical integration or sampling procedure, thereby dramatically improving computational efficiency for real-time selective inference.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Bootstrap for Post-Selection Inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from computational biology—the phylogenetic bootstrap—to the problem of inference after model selection in econometrics. In phylogenetics, bootstrap resampling of genetic sites is used to assign confidence values to branches in an evolutionary tree, which is itself the result of a complex model selection/optimization process. The analogous solution is to apply this bootstrap-after-selection logic to econometric models. Specifically, after an initial model is selected from data (e.g., via an automated procedure), one would generate bootstrap samples of the original data, re-apply the entire model selection algorithm to each sample, and then record the selected specifications. The resulting distribution of selected models, and the frequency of inclusion for parameters or functional forms, provides a direct, computationally intensive method for assessing parameter stability and constructing post-selection confidence intervals that account for the variability introduced by the selection step, moving beyond traditional asymptotic theory which often assumes a fixed, known model.

ideas.title: Robust Causal Adjustment with Integrated Sensitivity Analysis for Selection Bias
ideas.description: This approach combines modern causal inference with robust machine learning to model selection and outcomes while minimizing parametric assumptions. Its core innovation is the tight integration of a multi-dimensional sensitivity analysis that quantifies the robustness of causal estimates to violations in unmeasured confounding, model misspecification, and selection mechanism errors. The system outputs not just a point estimate, but a map of plausible estimates across a realistic space of assumption failures, providing a transparent measure of confidence and preventing overreliance on any single model.

ideas.title: Adapting Post-Hoc Calibration from Climate Ensemble Modeling for Selective Inference in Non-Linear Models
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from climate ensemble forecasting, where raw model outputs are statistically calibrated post-hoc to produce reliable uncertainty estimates. The parallel problem in statistics is providing valid inference after model selection for complex, non-linear models like trees and neural networks, where the selection process invalidates standard theory. The analogous solution is to adapt the ensemble calibration framework: treat the final selected model as one realization from an implicit ensemble of possible models generated by the selection algorithm. By analyzing the model's performance under perturbation (e.g., via bootstrap, jackknife, or a dedicated hold-out set), we can estimate and correct for the bias and overconfidence induced by selection. The goal is to create a post-processing layer that 'calibrates' p-values and confidence intervals, making them valid conditional on the selection event, much like climate scientists calibrate ensemble forecasts to be reliable probabilistic statements.

ideas.title: Prospective, Mechanistic Validation of Sensorimotor Model via Competitive Prediction in MS
ideas.description: This study moves beyond descriptive correlation to prospectively test the causal and predictive claims of the sensorimotor control model. A pre-registered analysis plan defines specific mechanistic predictions about future clinical decline based on baseline lesion connectivity profiles. These predictions are competitively tested against those from simpler, non-mechanistic benchmark models in a large, diverse, longitudinal MS cohort. The primary outcome is the superior explanatory and predictive power of the proposed model, rigorously evaluated using metrics like out-of-sample prediction error and Bayesian model comparison, thereby validating the model's utility for understanding disease progression.

ideas.title: Bootstrapped Lesion-Symptom Mapping for Robust Parameter Inference in Cognitive Models
ideas.description: This research direction integrates econometric bootstrapping with neuroimaging lesion analysis to assess the stability of cognitive model parameters linked to brain damage. By repeatedly resampling patient data and re-estimating model parameters from behavioral tasks, it generates bootstrap distributions for parameter values associated with specific lesion locations. This allows for inference on which parameter impairments (e.g., visual processing delay) are robustly tied to a lesion site, controlling for model selection variability and patient cohort heterogeneity. The result is a statistically rigorous, uncertainty-aware framework for lesion-symptom mapping that directly targets the computational mechanisms disrupted by brain injury.

ideas.title: Validate model using a stratified sampling and multi-site consortium approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from climate model intercomparison projects (like CMIP) to the challenge of validating a computational model in a heterogeneous disease population. In climate science, models are validated across diverse future scenarios and geographic regions through coordinated multi-group efforts with standardized protocols. Adapted here, the research would involve forming a multi-center consortium to apply the sensorimotor control model to a large, diverse MS cohort that is pre-stratified by disability level, lesion profile, and other key covariates. Using a standardized assessment protocol across sites, the model's output would be rigorously validated within each stratified subgroup. This approach moves beyond aggregate validation to identify the specific conditions and patient subpopulations where the model's predictions are robust, where it has systematic biases, and where it may fail, thereby precisely delineating its clinical and physiological applicability.

ideas.title: Post-Pipeline Inference: Valid Uncertainty Quantification After Automated Model Configuration
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for statistical inference after a complete automated machine learning (AutoML) or model configuration pipeline. It accounts for the compounded selection bias induced by the joint, data-driven choices of algorithm, hyperparameters, and pre-processing steps. The method constructs valid confidence intervals and p-values by conditioning on the event that the entire pipeline outputs a specific model configuration, generalizing selective inference to structured, multi-stage adaptive procedures. This provides principled uncertainty quantification for predictions and parameters from models tuned with complex, black-box optimization routines.

ideas.title: Selection-Aware Inference for Perturbation-Based Sensorimotor Feature Discovery
ideas.description: This research direction integrates high-precision robotic perturbation systems with advanced statistical inference methods that handle feature correlation post-selection. Robotic manipulanda apply structured, multi-dimensional perturbations to isolate proprioceptive and motor responses, generating a high-dimensional dataset of correlated neural and kinematic features. The novel selection-adjusted inference tools are then applied to this data to rigorously identify which perturbed features significantly drive observed neural or behavioral changes, accounting for the experimenter's adaptive choice of perturbation patterns based on ongoing results. This synergy enables a new paradigm of 'active causal probing' in sensorimotor neuroscience, where the perturbation protocol and statistical analysis are co-designed to reliably discover the latent structure of sensorimotor integration without inflation of false positive rates.

ideas.title: Joint Inference of Research Practices: Linking Textual Methodology to Statistical Footprints
ideas.description: This approach tackles the identifiability problem of inferring selection processes by integrating multiple streams of evidence. It trains a model on a corpus of published research where both the reported results (e.g., p-values, effect sizes) and textual descriptions of the methods are available. The system learns to associate patterns in reported statistics with specific methodological procedures described in the text, such as p-hacking, optional stopping, or pre-registration. This enables it to not only estimate the likely selection process from new results but also to quantify the consistency between a study's reported methods and its statistical output, providing a robust tool for meta-research and evidence assessment.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Signal Detection to Quantify Post-Selection Uncertainty
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from geophysical signal detection to the problem of quantifying predictive uncertainty post-model selection. In seismology, initial detections are validated with secondary statistical methods to confirm they are true events. Similarly, we propose a two-stage method where, after a model is selected via a standard procedure (first stage), a conformal prediction framework is deployed in a second stage, conditioned on the selection event. This adapted solution uses the held-out calibration data to construct prediction intervals that are valid conditional on the selected model, thereby rigorously quantifying the predictive uncertainty that remains after the selection process, which is often ignored. This provides a statistically sound confidence measure for predictions made with the chosen model.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Confidence-Ensemble Framework for Post-Selection Inference
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to integrate frequentist selective inference with Bayesian model averaging by drawing an analogy from climate science's multi-model ensemble forecasting. In that field, predictions from multiple global circulation models are combined into a weighted superensemble (using BMA-like techniques), which is then statistically post-processed to produce reliable, calibrated probabilistic forecasts. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to develop a hybrid confidence-ensemble framework for post-selection inference. The method would first use Bayesian Model Averaging to combine predictions from a set of candidate models, producing a weighted ensemble that quantifies model uncertainty. Subsequently, recognizing that constructing this ensemble constitutes a selection event from a larger model space, frequentist selective inference procedures would be applied. This conditions the final inference (confidence intervals or hypothesis tests for the ensemble-weighted parameters) on the selection, thereby yielding valid post-selection uncertainty quantification that coherently incorporates both Bayesian model uncertainty and frequentist selective coverage guarantees.

ideas.title: Online Selective Inference for Non-Stationary Data Streams with Change-Point Awareness
ideas.description: This research develops valid inferential methods for models selected on streaming data that explicitly account for and adapt to underlying distributional shifts. It integrates online change-point detection or hypothesis tests for stationarity directly into the model selection and inference pipeline, creating a joint framework. The approach provides confidence intervals and p-values that are conditionally valid not only given the model selection but also given the detected temporal structure of the data stream. This ensures robustness against real-world non-stationarity, a critical failure mode for standard online selective inference.

ideas.title: Pedagogical Scaffolds for Exact Selective Inference
ideas.description: This project develops an educational toolkit that makes exact, valid post-selection inference teachable and intuitive without resorting to invalid approximations. It centers on constrained interactive simulations where students explore model selection and hypothesis testing, but the underlying engine only computes exact p-values and confidence intervals via conditioning on the selection event. The scaffolding includes visual feedback linking the selection process to the adjusted sampling distributions, progressively introducing complexity while maintaining statistical validity as the core, non-negotiable lesson.

ideas.title: Efficient Selective Inference for Adaptive Systems with State-Dependent Feedback Delays
ideas.description: This research develops computationally efficient selective inference algorithms for systems where the feedback delay between action and sensory observation is not constant but varies based on system state or context. The core challenge is that the data-dependent selection event—which determines which hypotheses are tested—becomes a complex function of these variable delays, making the conditioning set for valid p-value calculation highly dynamic and historically expensive to compute. By creating novel approximations and algorithmic shortcuts tailored to the structure of state-dependent delay models, this work aims to provide fast, valid confidence intervals and p-values for parameters in adaptive control, robotics, or neuroscience experiments, where both selection and sensory processing times are in flux.

ideas.description: This research direction fuses the study of failed compensation for visual delays in MS with distributed computing for selection-adjusted inference. It proposes to model the brain's adaptive mechanisms as a large, distributed computational network. By running massive, parallel simulations of candidate compensation models on neuroimaging and behavioral datasets, the project can systematically test which model parameters or network pathways most likely explain the observed failure. The key innovation is using distributed algorithms to compute valid, post-selection confidence intervals across this vast model space, providing statistically robust identification of the specific neural or computational loci where compensation breaks down, moving beyond correlation to causal inference in complex neurological dysfunction.

ideas.title: Adopt Paleobiology's Fossil Record Correction for Causal Selection Bias
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from paleobiology, where scientists correct for severe selection bias in the fossil record to estimate true historical biodiversity. The analogous solution uses subsampling and model-based techniques (e.g., rarefaction, shareholder quorum subsampling) to adjust for non-random preservation and sampling effort. Adapted to causal inference, this involves treating the observed, selectively sampled data as a biased 'fossil record' of potential outcomes. The research would develop a framework to apply analogous correction methods—such as constructing bias-aware subsamples or developing weights that account for 'preservation probability' (i.e., selection probability)—to reconstruct unbiased counterfactual distributions, thereby enabling valid causal effect estimation from non-randomly selected observational data.

ideas.title: Bootstrapped Inference for Proprioceptive Parameter Stability Under Robotic Perturbation
ideas.description: This research direction combines the precise, repeatable mechanical perturbations delivered by robotic exoskeletons with econometric bootstrapping techniques for statistical inference. The core idea is to apply bootstrap resampling methods to the time-series data of limb kinematics and forces generated during robotic perturbation protocols, which are designed to isolate proprioceptive feedback dynamics. This allows for the empirical estimation of confidence intervals and hypothesis testing regarding the stability of identified sensorimotor model parameters (e.g., stiffness, damping, reflex gains) across different experimental conditions or model architectures. The synergy provides a rigorous, data-driven statistical tool to validate which models of proprioceptive processing remain stable and generalizable despite the inherent variability and model selection challenges in perturbation-based system identification.

ideas.title: State-Dependent Delay Phenotyping for Personalized Tremor Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the modeling of variable, state-dependent sensory delays with machine learning-based tremor classification to create a novel phenotyping framework. Instead of clustering on fixed model parameters, we will train dynamical models where feedback delays are functions of limb state (e.g., position, velocity) or tremor phase, and then use unsupervised learning to cluster patients based on these learned delay-state relationships. This approach hypothesizes that distinct physiological subtypes of MS tremor are characterized by unique patterns of how neural transmission delays change with movement, potentially linking subtypes to specific underlying pathologies in conduction or processing. The resulting personalized, delay-aware models could then inform the development of subtype-specific neuromodulation or robotic therapy strategies that adapt to the patient's real-time delay dynamics.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop System Identification of Lesion-Specific Sensorimotor Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates aerospace-inspired system identification with lesion-deficit mapping to create a novel computational neurology tool. Advanced parameter estimation techniques are used to fit a sensorimotor control model to behavioral data from patients with focal brain lesions, but the search space is rigorously constrained by prior probabilities derived from the lesion's location and its known impact on neural processing pathways. This yields a generative model that not only predicts behavior but also infers the specific functional disruptions—such as increased noise, delay, or gain change—caused by the anatomical damage. The result is a quantifiable, mechanistic link between brain structure and dynamic control function, moving beyond correlation to a causal, model-based explanation of neurological deficits.

ideas.title: Forensic Statistics: Inferring the Analysis Workflow from the Published P-value Distribution
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from geology to the original inverse problem of recovering a selection process from results. In sedimentology, the end-member modeling analysis (EMMA) technique is used to infer the history of sediment transport and deposition (the selection process) from the final grain size distribution (the results). We adapt this by treating the distribution of reported p-values in scientific literature as the final 'sediment.' This distribution is modeled as a mixture of theoretical distributions, each representing a canonical research selection behavior (e.g., the uniform distribution of honest nulls, the right-skewed distribution from p-hacking, the spike near 0.05 from selective reporting, and the truncation from publication bias). By fitting a mixture model to observed p-value data, we can statistically decompose it to infer the likely prevalence and combination of these selection procedures that generated the published results, providing a quantitative forensic tool for meta-research.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference of Sensorimotor Selection Policies from Perturbation Response Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction combines robotic exoskeletons for applying precise, multi-dimensional mechanical perturbations with statistical inverse problem techniques. The core idea is to treat the observed motor corrections and adaptations—measured as changes in force, kinematics, or neural signals—as the 'reported results' analogous to p-values. By analyzing the dynamic response profile across many systematically varied perturbations, we can mathematically invert the observed data to infer the likely internal selection process the sensorimotor system uses, such as which proprioceptive feedback channels are prioritized, how error signals are gated, or which internal model is recruited for stabilization. This moves beyond merely describing behavior to formally identifying the causal computational policies governing sensorimotor selection.

ideas.title: Voxel-Based Lesion-Symptom Mapping for Computational Model Parameters
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Voxel-Based Lesion-Symptom Mapping (VLSM) from cognitive neuropsychology to the context of linking model parameters to neuroimaging lesion data. In the analogous field, VLSM statistically correlates quantified behavioral deficits with lesion location on a voxel-by-voxel basis across patients to pinpoint brain regions necessary for specific cognitive functions. The adapted research idea is to treat impaired parameters of a computational cognitive model (e.g., visual delay, synaptic weight, noise variance) fitted to individual patient data as the quantitative 'symptoms'. By performing a mass-univariate statistical analysis correlating the value of each model parameter with the presence/absence of a lesion in each brain voxel across a patient cohort, one can generate statistical maps identifying which brain regions, when damaged, are associated with specific alterations in the model's functional architecture.

ideas.title: Mapping the Sensorimotor Efficiency Frontier to Quantify Human Strategy Optimality
ideas.description: This research program directly tests the optimality of the healthy human control model by first defining a multi-objective efficiency space for specific sensorimotor tasks, encompassing metrics like accuracy, metabolic cost, and noise robustness. It then employs high-throughput computational optimization (e.g., deep RL) on biologically plausible musculoskeletal simulators to discover the full set of viable control strategies, mapping the theoretical efficiency frontier. The performance and characteristics of the healthy human model are then quantitatively compared to this frontier, identifying which axes it saturates and where alternative, non-biological strategies prove superior. This yields concrete evidence on whether human control is globally optimal or a contingent, evolved solution bounded by phylogenetic history and anatomical constraints.

ideas.title: Pedagogical Selective Inference for Validating Neuroimaging-Based Delay Estimates
ideas.description: This fused idea develops an intuitive, simplified selective inference toolkit specifically designed for educational use in neuroscience and psychology curricula. The toolkit uses the concrete problem of estimating visual and proprioceptive feedback delays from EEG/MEG data as its central case study. It teaches students how to perform valid statistical inference after selecting neural features, latency windows, or sensor clusters based on the same data used for testing, a common pitfall in neuroimaging. This approach grounds abstract statistical concepts in a tangible neuroscientific question, enabling learners to both understand post-selection inference and correctly interpret the uncertainty in empirically measured neural delays.

ideas.title: Parallel Multi-Hypothesis Testing via Distributed Ray Tracing Architecture
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from distributed ray tracing in computer graphics to scale the computation of selection-adjusted confidence intervals. In ray tracing, the image is tiled, and nodes render tiles in parallel, later communicating to resolve global lighting effects—analogous to local statistical tests needing adjustment for global selection. We propose partitioning a large dataset across a distributed system, where nodes compute local test statistics and selection indicators. A subsequent coordinated aggregation phase, using communication patterns from distributed rendering, combines this information to apply the necessary post-selection correction across the entire dataset, thereby parallelizing the computationally intensive adjustment process while maintaining statistical validity.
