ideas.description: This approach moves beyond correlation by causally testing model parameter mappings. It uses targeted TMS over candidate cortical regions in healthy volunteers to induce temporary, focal disruptions, creating precise 'virtual lesions' and measuring the resulting behavioral deficits. The same computational model is then fitted to both these TMS-induced deficits and to behavioral data from patients with real anatomical lesions (e.g., from stroke). The system robustly identifies a model parameter as linked to a brain region only if it is reliably perturbed by both the virtual lesion in the healthy brain and the anatomical lesion in patients, establishing convergent causal evidence.

ideas.title: Challenging the Healthy Control Model as the Optimal Baseline via Ecological Niche Theory
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that healthy control subjects represent the singular, optimal baseline for sensorimotor control models. Drawing an analogy from Ecology and Evolutionary Biology, where the notion of a single 'optimal' organism was superseded by niche theory recognizing multiple efficient strategies (generalists, specialists), we adapt this solution. The adapted research conceptualizes different sensorimotor tasks and environments as distinct 'niches.' It proposes to empirically test whether the control group's strategy is just one efficient strategy adapted to the typical lab niche, and to identify and model alternative efficient control strategies that may be optimal for different niches, thereby reframing the healthy control model as a specific, rather than universal, benchmark.

ideas.title: Robotic Perturbation-Based Mapping of Non-Normative Sensorimotor Control Baselines
ideas.description: This research program challenges the assumption that healthy control models represent the only or optimal baseline for sensorimotor control. It employs high-precision robotic exoskeletons to apply controlled mechanical perturbations, not just to study known control strategies, but to actively search for and induce latent, non-normative control solutions in both healthy and clinical populations. By mapping how different nervous systems (e.g., post-stroke, with neuropathy) discover and stabilize alternative feedback dynamics under perturbation, we can identify a spectrum of viable control baselines. This reveals fundamental principles of sensorimotor flexibility and may inform rehabilitation by targeting the acquisition of newly discovered, efficient strategies rather than forcing a return to a presumed 'normal' model.

ideas.description: This research applies and extends recursive system identification techniques, such as Recursive Least Squares with variable forgetting factors, to continuously estimate and update sensorimotor feedback model parameters in real-time. A Bayesian change-point detection module monitors estimation residuals to signal significant physiological shifts, triggering a model re-initialization or increased adaptation rate. This creates a closed-loop system identification process that maintains model fidelity despite the inherent non-stationarity of the biological plant. The outcome is a robust, self-correcting model essential for reliable long-term operation of adaptive neuroprosthetics or personalized rehabilitation systems.

ideas.description: This idea adapts advanced system identification techniques from geophysical subsurface imaging to refine sensorimotor feedback models. In geophysics, seismic inversion uses surface measurements of seismic waves to iteratively identify the hidden internal structure of the Earth by adjusting a high-dimensional model of subsurface properties. Analogously, we can treat recorded neural and kinematic signals from a biological system as the observable output. By applying similar iterative, model-based optimization algorithms, we can adjust the parameters of a complex sensorimotor model (including reflex gains, delays, and activation dynamics) to minimize the difference between simulated and experimental data, thereby 'imaging' the internal control parameters of the sensorimotor feedback system with high precision.

The analogous problem is teaching about false patterns in random visual data, solved using hands-on demonstrations of selection from noise to build intuitive understanding of inflated false discovery. Adapted to statistical pedagogy, the research idea involves creating an interactive, visual teaching module. Students first engage in a 'fishing expedition' by selecting apparent patterns from simulated noisy data, then the module uses approximate, intuitive methods (like simple simulation or a visual 'inference band' that widens with selection) to demonstrate the necessity and concept of post-selection inference, providing a concrete, experiential foundation before formal theory.

ideas.title: Test for a Generalized Sensorimotor Timing Deficit Across Modalities and Effectors
ideas.description: This idea expands the original challenge to the assumption of task specificity by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuropsychology. Research on cerebellar ataxia solved the analogous problem of distinguishing a general timing deficit from motor-specific incoordination by testing patients across a battery of tasks that varied sensory modality and motor effector. Adapting this solution, the research would test if the identified visual delay and dynamics misestimation are general deficits by employing a multi-modal, multi-effector task battery. Participants would perform timing and prediction tasks using visual, auditory, and tactile cues to guide movements of the elbow, wrist, hand, eyes, and even vocal responses. A consistent impairment across these diverse conditions would strongly indicate a core deficit in sensorimotor timing or internal model prediction, rather than a deficit specific to the visual control of the elbow.

ideas.title: Model sensory delays as a dynamic, context-dependent filter
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of a single, fixed feedback delay by proposing to model sensory delays as variable and state-dependent, analogous to how network engineering handles variable latency in TCP. The analogous solution from TCP uses a dynamic Round-Trip Time estimator that continuously samples and smoothes delay measurements, adapting protocol behavior to current network conditions. Adapted to neuroscience and robotics, this involves creating a neural or computational model where an adaptive filter continuously estimates the current sensory feedback delay based on recent signal history and its variance. This dynamic delay estimate would be fed into predictive models (like internal models or Kalman filters) for state estimation and motor control, allowing the system to maintain stability and performance even as transmission latencies fluctuate due to attention, stimulus complexity, or neural noise.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Waveform Cross-Correlation to Measure Neural Feedback Delays
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to the neuroscience problem of measuring sensory feedback delays. In seismology, cross-correlation of waveforms from distributed sensors is used to measure precise time delays of seismic waves, enabling velocity calculations in complex media. The adapted research idea applies this methodological principle to EEG/MEG neuroimaging. By presenting a time-locked sensory event (visual or proprioceptive) and performing high-resolution cross-correlation analysis on the evoked responses recorded from relevant, spatially distinct cortical regions, researchers can directly compute the relative processing latency between pathways. This offers a direct empirical measurement to validate and refine existing model-based estimates of visual and proprioceptive feedback delays, providing a data-driven tool for studying sensorimotor integration and temporal processing in the brain.

ideas.title: A Coupled Multi-Joint Framework for Modeling Task-Dependent Tremor Propagation in the Upper Limb
ideas.description: This research develops a unified modeling framework to study tremor not as an isolated joint phenomenon, but as a dynamically coordinated activity across the shoulder, elbow, and wrist. The core innovation is a hierarchical control architecture embedded within a scalable musculoskeletal model, allowing the simulation of how tremor manifests during different functional tasks like reaching or posture holding. This approach directly tests hypotheses about neural coupling and mechanical resonance between joints, providing a systems-level explanation for tremor characteristics that single-joint models cannot capture.

ideas.title: Adapting Geophysical Seismic Tomography for Proprioceptive Mapping via Robotic Perturbations
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of geophysical seismic tomography to the problem of isolating proprioceptive feedback dynamics. In seismology, controlled sources generate waves whose propagation through heterogeneous media is measured to image subsurface structure. Analogously, a robotic exoskeleton will be used as a programmable 'perturbation source' to apply precise, spatially and temporally varied mechanical perturbations to a human joint or limb. The resulting high-fidelity kinematic and electromyographic response data will be fed into computational inversion algorithms, adapted from tomographic methods, to reconstruct a functional map of the proprioceptive system's dynamics. This allows for the isolation of specific feedback pathways (e.g., muscle spindles vs. Golgi tendon organs) by their distinct 'signatures' in the perturbation-response matrix, moving beyond single-perturbation analysis to a systems-level identification approach.

ideas.title: Closed-loop computational disentanglement of cerebellar-driven vs. sensory-contingent tremor dynamics
ideas.description: This project develops a hybrid computational-experimental framework to rigorously test the primacy of cerebellar circuit dysfunction in tremor generation. A biologically constrained cerebellar model is integrated into a closed-loop sensorimotor simulation, where 'cerebellar' and 'sensory' perturbations can be independently applied and their dynamic interactions quantified. The system uses model-based predictions to design real-world experiments that selectively validate or refute the cerebellar-driver hypothesis, moving beyond correlative observations to establish causal, mechanistic precedence in tremor pathogenesis.

ideas.title: Applying Cardiac Pacemaker Theory to Cerebellar Oscillator Dysfunction in Tremor
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that tremor is purely a sensorimotor feedback disorder by drawing an analogy from cardiology. In cardiac electrophysiology, arrhythmias were once attributed mainly to conduction problems, but the identification and treatment of intrinsic pacemaker dysfunction (e.g., ectopic foci, sick sinus syndrome) revolutionized the field. Adapting this solution, we propose to investigate whether primary cerebellar circuit dysfunction involves specific neuronal ensembles developing pathological autonomous oscillatory activity, effectively acting as a 'tremor pacemaker'. The adapted research would use tools from systems neuroscience to identify, characterize, and selectively target these putative cerebellar pacemaker regions in tremor models, testing if their dysfunction is a primary driver, not merely a consequence of impaired sensory feedback.

ideas.title: Multisensory Robotic Perturbation Platform for Studying Sensorimotor Integration Dynamics
ideas.description: This research system employs a high-precision robotic exoskeleton or manipulandum integrated with a head-mounted virtual reality display and a motion platform to apply controlled mechanical perturbations. Crucially, it simultaneously delivers precisely calibrated visual and vestibular stimuli that can be either congruent or incongruent with the proprioceptive perturbation. This design allows investigators to probe how the central nervous system weights and integrates conflicting sensory signals during dynamic motor corrections, moving beyond isolated proprioception. The approach reveals the adaptive, context-dependent algorithms governing sensorimotor feedback, providing a more ecologically valid and powerful model of real-world balance and manipulation.
